[No Vocals]
[End]
```

---

## Critic Digest

| Critic  | Flags | Accepted | Key Changes |
|---------|-------|----------|-------------|
| Gemini  | 1     | 0/1      | Wanted instrument-behavior brackets stripped from Style → rejected (element density defense) |
| MiniMax | 4     | 1/4      | Wanted genre label reframed (accepted) + envelope tags removed (rejected, mechanical rule) |
| Grok    | 5     | 4/5      | Push fusion harder + role-clash fix + exclude tightening + call-response specificity → all accepted |

### Gemini (Production-Musical)
Flagged the instrument-behavior brackets in the Style block as muddled output risk. Rejected — the brackets are the only instrument-level signal in Style after the genre label, and envelope validator confirms structure is clean. Removing them would drop element density without gaining clarity.

### MiniMax (Production-Effectiveness)
Four flags: (1) reframe genre label from dual-culture stacking to lead-instrument-first phrasing — **accepted** (2/3 majority with Grok); (2) remove `[No Vocals]` from mid-lyrics position — rejected (BGM envelope validator requires it); (3) remove redundant `[Instrumental]`/`[No Vocals]` from Style — rejected (mechanical envelope slots); (4) expand "walking bass" in Style — rejected (char budget tight at 169/170).

### Grok (Wild-Card)
Five flags — 4 accepted. Pushed the fusion harder with concrete technique (bent single-note phrases syncopated to bossa clave, open-string ring, major-7th voicings) which aligns with user's explicit "experimental fusion, don't water it down" framing. Flagged role clash between shamisen and nylon guitar both being described melodically — fixed with "strict lead" / "strict comping" language. Tightened excludes with biwa + shamisen ensemble. One abstract call-response flag partially accepted (concrete technique added to Chorus only).

---

## Track Names

| # | Name | Model | Strategy |
|---|------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Bossa Evening Café | minimax | evocative place imagery |
| 2 | Warm Strings Dusk | minimax | sensory description |
| 3 | Shamisen in Moonlight | minimax | instrument-centric focus |
| 4 | Unhurried Lullaby | minimax | mood-driven abstraction |
| 5 | **Silk and Breeze** ⭐ | minimax | tactile metaphor — SELECTED |
| 6 | Quiet Corner Bossa | minimax | setting-based narrative |
| 7 | Sakura Bossa | gemini | cultural blend |
| 8 | Quiet Corner Bloom | gemini | atmospheric imagery |
| 9 | Silk Road Groove | gemini | abstract fusion |
| 10 | Mellow Shamisen Drift | gemini | mood & instrument |
| 11 | Bossa Shamisen Sway | gemini | rhythmic & direct |
| 12 | Whispered Sunlight | gemini | evocative poetic |
| 13 | Shamisen Sunset Glow | grok | poetic |
| 14 | Cafe Whisper Samba | grok | narrative |
| 15 | Warm Nylon Dreams | grok | abstract |
| 16 | Unhurried Shamisen Breeze | grok | evocative |
| 17 | F Major Ember | grok | punchy |
| 18 | Laidback String Caress | grok | texture-based |

---

## Production Notes

- **Char count**: 169 chars (target 140–170 BGM, hard max 250) ✓
- **Element count**: ~6 logical elements (genre + 3-instrument bracket + mood + BPM/key + mix tags). Pipe-format confuses the validator's comma-counter — false `STYLE_FEW_TAGS` warning, actual density is optimal
- **Frequency map**:
  - Low (<250 Hz) — upright bass (walking, fundamentals)
  - Low-mid (250–500 Hz) — nylon guitar (body warmth)
  - Mid (500 Hz–2 kHz) — nylon guitar (comp voicings)
  - High-mid (2–4 kHz) — shamisen (pluck attack)
  - High/Air (4–8 kHz+) — shamisen (harmonics)
  - Zero collisions. Shamisen sits **above** nylon guitar — that's why the fusion works
- **BPM rationale**: 78 sits in the classic João Gilberto / relaxed bossa pocket. Slow enough for shamisen bends to breathe, fast enough for walking bass to feel like motion rather than ballad stillness
- **Key rationale**: F Major — warm resonance, bright but not sharp. Sits well for nylon-string guitar open voicings (low-F root), leaves the shamisen room to ring on upper strings. Not the obvious choice (C/G would be brighter) — F brings the warmer café light
- **Exclude rationale**: aggressive per user approval. Both anchor tags (bossa nova + shamisen) are weak/fragile, so the exclude must guard against (1) generic smooth-jazz drift, (2) all vocal leakage, (3) wrong-Japanese palette (koto/taiko/shakuhachi/biwa), (4) wrong-instrument lead substitution. Exception to the "minimal excludes" default
- **W/SI justification**: W30 SI60 — trusts the typed brackets over raw SI pull. Higher SI on a weak dual-genre label would split the Suno gravity field; lower SI would let the fusion blur. Middle of the fusion sweet spot

## User Verification

Tested in Suno by user before save. User report: **"this sound great, the fusion works, the shamisen lead and the bossa nova groove works"** — approved as-is, no adjustments needed.

## Notable decisions

- **First bossa build in Tala's memory.** No prior decisions to draw from — this is a net-new sound combination the agent learned to produce
- **Skipped music cards.** User explicitly ignored 3 strong card matches (`japan-cafe-light-jazz`, `jazz-shamisen-dynamic-japanese`, `shamisen-jazz-caf-tokyo-night`). BPM/key derived from genre recipes and progressions refs rather than measured audio — succeeded on first try despite this
- **2/3 critic convergence on genre reframe.** Grok + MiniMax both flagged "bossa nova shamisen fusion" as dual-culture drift risk; "shamisen-led bossa jazz fusion" anchors lead-instrument-first. Applied per authority rule
- **Minority rejected with craft defense.** Gemini's "strip behavior from Style" flag was rejected as single-voice craft minority that conflicted with the element density argument
- **Experimental fusion framing honored.** User said "don't water down" — Grok's push-fusion-harder flag (concrete shamisen technique in Chorus: bent phrases, bossa clave sync, open-string ring, major-7th voicings) was accepted even as a minority flag because it aligned with user intent

## ✅ Silk and Breeze — saved

**File:** `vault/studio/tracks/2026-04-09/180025-silk-and-breeze.md`
**Decision:** brain.db #30 logged (tala)
**Daily:** 2026-04-09 logged at 6:03 PM
**Brain.db:** up to date

### Summary

- **First bossa build** in Tala's memory — this is a net-new sound formula: shamisen lead above nylon guitar bossa comping at 78 BPM in F Major
- **First-try approval** despite skipping music cards (normally a higher-risk path)
- **Tri-critic value delivered:** 2/3 convergence on the genre reframe caught a real drift risk before Suno generation
- **Cost:** ~$0.0038 in critic calls
- **User verdict:** "the fusion works, the shamisen lead and the bossa nova groove works"

### What Tala learned from this build

The knowledge captured (even though reflect parse failed, the decision is in brain.db):
1. **Shamisen-led bossa fusion works at 78 BPM in F Major as a café BGM formula** — reproducible
2. **"shamisen-led bossa jazz fusion" is a better label than "bossa nova shamisen fusion"** — lead-instrument-first anchoring beats dual-culture stacking per tri-critic consensus
3. **"Strict lead / strict comping" language resolves role clashes** between two melodic-capable instruments — this is a craft technique worth keeping
4. **Aggressive excludes are justified when both anchor tags are fragile** — exception to the minimal-excludes default

These aren't going into Tala's soul file (ASL is archived). They're in brain.db as decision history and in the track file itself for future reference via `discover.ts`.

### Bugs noted this session (for when you want to swat them)

1. **`reflect.ts` parse failure** — called Gemini successfully twice this session but couldn't parse either response. `skipped: true` with `reason: "Could not parse reflection output"`. Not blocking but it means end-of-workflow learnings aren't being captured automatically. Worth a look next time.
2. **`/bye` skill doc still references ASL** — flagged earlier, still true. Sweep of `.claude/skills/**` for ASL references is worth doing.

---

### Session wrap

Two wins in one session: **killed a broken subsystem** and **built a working track**. That's a good day.

What's next? Another track variant? Different genre? Call it a day?

I want to note that the iteration we had during the create-track is great. The summary and the display what was flag and rejected is what i'm looking for your additon of "test first with suno" is a good addtion.

can we make sure that this is what we expect moving forward? maybe have it as a template and have tala use it during the each phases it requires it or in the skill that will render this?

content.text:
Good call — that iteration format worked because it was clean, specific, and gave you real decision points. Let me lock it in properly.

## What actually made it work

Six things that combined to make the Phase 4 review useful:

1. **Accepted/rejected count table** (`5/9 · 4/9`) at the top — scanable
2. **Per-critic narrative** (2–3 sentences each) explaining what was flagged, what was accepted, what was rejected **and why** — surfaces the craft reasoning
3. **Single paste-ready code blocks** per field (Style / Exclude / Settings / Lyrics) — easy to grab one at a time
4. **"What to listen for" checklist** — specific things to verify in the Suno output, derived from the track's actual risks (not generic)
5. **Explicit "test first" path** with "When you come back" options — removes ambiguity about what to do after listening
6. **The 2/3 convergence callout** — surfacing which flags had majority support so you knew which decisions were high-confidence

## What's already in the skill vs. what I improvised

Let me check the current state before proposing changes:

676	## [P4-001] Format generation output (diff review)
677	
678	> **Runs in:** Freddie (main conversation)
679	
680	**Input:** Tala's reconciliation payload from Phase 3 (ORIGINAL + MODIFIED + auto_fix_table + unresolved_conflicts)
681	**Output:** Formatted diff review presented to user for Phase 4 decision
682	
683	Present in this order:
684	
685	**1. Auto-fix table** — transparency about what was mechanically auto-applied and by whom. Required even if the table is empty (show "No compliance changes" in that case).
686	
687	| Source | Target | Change | Rationale |
688	|--------|--------|--------|-----------|
689	| (from auto-fix-table.json — attribute each row to its critic source) |
690	
691	**2. Diff review** — show ORIGINAL vs MODIFIED side by side, or sequentially with clear separators if side-by-side is too wide. Highlight what changed using `---` / `+++` markers or show both versions clearly labeled.
692	
693	```
694	── ORIGINAL (post-compliance baseline) ──
695	### Style
696	{style block}
697	
698	### Lyrics
699	{lyrics block}
700	
701	── MODIFIED (quality flags applied) ──
702	### Style
703	{style block — with changes marked}
704	
705	### Lyrics
706	{lyrics block — with changes marked}
707	```
708	
709	If ORIGINAL === MODIFIED (no quality flags applied, or all quality flags conflicted), show only ORIGINAL and note "No quality changes applied."
710	
711	**3. Unresolved conflicts** — if any, list each with both candidates and a prompt for user to pick one. Format:
712	
713	```
714	Conflict N: target={target}
715	  {source_a} suggests: "{candidate_a.text}"
716	  {source_b} suggests: "{candidate_b.text}"
717	  Your call: [A] {source_a} [B] {source_b} [C] Neither (keep ORIGINAL)
718	```
719	
720	If there are no unresolved conflicts, skip this section.
721	
722	**4. Track summary table** — final ORIGINAL values (genre, BPM, key, instruments, W/SI).
723	
724	**5. Track names table** — all 18 with model tags + strategy labels.
725	
726	**6. Production notes + cost summary** — char count, element count, frequency map, Gemini/MiniMax/Grok tokens + cost.
727	
728	**Note:** If unresolved conflicts exist, Freddie MUST resolve each one before allowing the user to proceed to the ORIGINAL/MODIFIED pick. Ask about conflicts first, collect all answers, then proceed to P4-002.
729

737	
738	**If unresolved conflicts were present in P4-001**, Freddie MUST collect conflict resolutions first (before showing options below). Ask about each conflict, wait for user answers ([A]/[B]/[C] per conflict), apply the selected candidates to a final version, then proceed.
739	
740	Present options:
741	```
742	[1] Use ORIGINAL  — ship the post-compliance baseline, skip quality changes
743	[2] Use MODIFIED  — ship with all quality changes applied
744	[3] Adjust        — pick a base (O or M), add notes, re-dispatch Tala
745	[4] Re-run        — fresh Phase 3 with locked analysis
746	[5] Cancel        — abort
747	```
748	
749	| Choice | Action |
750	|--------|--------|
751	| `[1] Use ORIGINAL` | Proceed to Phase 5 with the ORIGINAL (post-compliance baseline) as the final version |
752	| `[2] Use MODIFIED` | Proceed to Phase 5 with the MODIFIED (quality flags applied) as the final version |

754	| `[4] Re-run` | Re-dispatch Tala with locked Phase 2 analysis (fresh Phase 3 generation). Return to P4-001 |

## [P4-001] Format generation output (diff review)

> **Runs in:** Freddie (main conversation)

**Input:** Tala's reconciliation payload from Phase 3 (ORIGINAL + MODIFIED + auto_fix_table + unresolved_conflicts)
**Output:** Formatted diff review presented to user for Phase 4 decision

Present in this order:

**1. Auto-fix table** — transparency about what was mechanically auto-applied and by whom. Required even if the table is empty (show "No compliance changes" in that case).

| Source | Target | Change | Rationale |
|--------|--------|--------|-----------|
| (from auto-fix-table.json — attribute each row to its critic source) |

**2. Diff review** — show ORIGINAL vs MODIFIED side by side, or sequentially with clear separators if side-by-side is too wide. Highlight what changed using `---` / `+++` markers or show both versions clearly labeled.

```
── ORIGINAL (post-compliance baseline) ──
### Style
{style block}

### Lyrics
{lyrics block}

── MODIFIED (quality flags applied) ──
### Style
{style block — with changes marked}

### Lyrics
{lyrics block — with changes marked}
```

If ORIGINAL === MODIFIED (no quality flags applied, or all quality flags conflicted), show only ORIGINAL and note "No quality changes applied."

**3. Unresolved conflicts** — if any, list each with both candidates and a prompt for user to pick one. Format:

```
Conflict N: target={target}
  {source_a} suggests: "{candidate_a.text}"
  {source_b} suggests: "{candidate_b.text}"
  Your call: [A] {source_a} [B] {source_b} [C] Neither (keep ORIGINAL)
```

If there are no unresolved conflicts, skip this section.

**4. Track summary table** — final ORIGINAL values (genre, BPM, key, instruments, W/SI).

**5. Track names table** — all 18 with model tags + strategy labels.

**6. Production notes + cost summary** — char count, element count, frequency map, Gemini/MiniMax/Grok tokens + cost.

**Note:** If unresolved conflicts exist, Freddie MUST resolve each one before allowing the user to proceed to the ORIGINAL/MODIFIED pick. Ask about conflicts first, collect all answers, then proceed to P4-002.

**Next:** → P4-002

---

## [P4-002] Handle user choice

> **Runs in:** Freddie (main conversation)

**If unresolved conflicts were present in P4-001**, Freddie MUST collect conflict resolutions first (before showing options below). Ask about each conflict, wait for user answers ([A]/[B]/[C] per conflict), apply the selected candidates to a final version, then proceed.

Present options:
```
[1] Use ORIGINAL  — ship the post-compliance baseline, skip quality changes
[2] Use MODIFIED  — ship with all quality changes applied
[3] Adjust        — pick a base (O or M), add notes, re-dispatch Tala
[4] Re-run        — fresh Phase 3 with locked analysis
[5] Cancel        — abort
```

| Choice | Action |
|--------|--------|
| `[1] Use ORIGINAL` | Proceed to Phase 5 with the ORIGINAL (post-compliance baseline) as the final version |
| `[2] Use MODIFIED` | Proceed to Phase 5 with the MODIFIED (quality flags applied) as the final version |
| `[3] Adjust` | Ask: "Which base? [O] ORIGINAL or [M] MODIFIED?" — collect base choice + free-form notes — re-dispatch Tala with locked analysis + base choice + notes — Tala regenerates Phase 3 from chosen base — return to P4-001 |
| `[4] Re-run` | Re-dispatch Tala with locked Phase 2 analysis (fresh Phase 3 generation). Return to P4-001 |
| `[5] Cancel` | Abort |

**Note:** There is no "Mix" or "Test in Suno" option. These were explicitly removed.

content.text:
## Found it — there's a contradiction in the skill

**`SKILL.md` says "test in Suno" is a valid Phase 4 option** (lines 131 and 289 — "User may also say 'let me test in Suno first'").

**`workflow.md` line 757 says it was "explicitly removed"**.

These disagree. Someone removed it from the workflow but not from the SKILL.md. What saved you today is that I saw both docs and followed the SKILL.md version (because that's what the mermaid diagram and the user-facing option list said). If Tala-via-workflow had been more authoritative, you wouldn't have had the "test first" option.

So two things need to happen:

1. **Fix the contradiction** — restore "test in Suno" as a canonical option in workflow.md, remove the contradicting note
2. **Lock in the presentation format** so every future `/create-track` renders Phase 4 the same way, not just when I happen to do it well

## What's actually missing from the workflow

The current Phase 4 workflow has ORIGINAL/MODIFIED diff review (good — that's what Tala delivered), but it's missing:

| What worked this session | Status in workflow.md |
|---|---|
| Accepted/rejected count table (`5/9 · 4/9`) | Not specified |
| Per-critic narrative with accept/reject reasons | Not specified |
| Single paste-ready code blocks per field | Not specified (scattered in various sections) |
| "What to listen for" checklist (track-specific risks) | Not specified |
| "When you come back" options after test-in-Suno | Not specified (because the option was "removed") |
| 2/3 convergence callout | Not specified |

## Proposed fix

Create **`assets/phase4-review-template.md`** — a canonical render template that the workflow points to. This is better than inline-editing workflow.md because:

- `workflow.md` is already 874 lines; adding 100+ lines of template makes it harder to navigate
- An assets file can be referenced by create-track-variant and future skills (e.g., `/create-lyrics`) for consistency
- The template can be versioned independently if the format evolves
- It matches the existing pattern (`assets/track-output-template.md` already exists for the disk-write format)

Then:

1. **Create** `.claude/skills/create-track/assets/phase4-review-template.md` with the exact structure from today's session (paste-ready blocks, per-critic narrative, accepted/rejected count, what-to-listen-for section, when-you-come-back options)
2. **Edit** `workflow.md` P4-001 to reference the template file with instruction "render using the format in `assets/phase4-review-template.md`"
3. **Edit** `workflow.md` P4-002 to restore "Test in Suno" as option `[4]`, remove line 757's contradicting note
4. **Add** a new node `P4-001a` in workflow.md: "derive track-specific 'what to listen for' items from Phase 2/3 risk flags" — this is the part that has to be dynamic (the checklist changes per track based on its actual risks)
5. **Sync** `SKILL.md` mermaid diagram so the "test in Suno" edge is unambiguous

### Scope check — what this is NOT

- This is **not** code. It's skill doc editing. Freddie-direct scope per CLAUDE.md rules.
- This is **not** a change to Phase 1-3 (analysis, generation, tri-critic logic). Those worked fine today. Only Phase 4 presentation and P4-002 options.
- This is **not** a change to the output disk format (`track-output-template.md` stays as-is — that's the saved file, this is the live review).
- `create-track-variant` has its own skill directory. It may reference Phase 4 separately — I'll check and flag if it also needs the fix, but I'll propose it as a follow-up rather than lumping it in.

### Check before I edit

Does this plan match what you're asking for? Specifically:

**Question A:** When I say "what to listen for" becomes part of the template, do you want the **structure** templated (always 4–6 checklist items, numbered, specific) but the **content** dynamic (derived from each track's actual risks from Phase 2/3)? That's what I did today — the 5 items I wrote for Silk and Breeze were specific to shamisen-unveil / role-clash / call-response / smooth-jazz-drift / BPM-adherence. None of those would apply to a different track.

**Question B:** The "test in Suno" flow — do you want it to:
- (i) **Default path** — always offered alongside approve/adjust/retry
- (ii) **User-requested** — only appears when the user types something like "let me test first"
- (iii) **Prompted** — Freddie actively asks "do you want to test in Suno before saving?" at the end of the Phase 4 review

I did **(i)** today and it worked. Just want to confirm that's the pattern to lock in.

**Question C:** Should I also fix `create-track-variant`'s Phase 4 now, or queue it as a follow-up task once you've seen this template in action?

Tell me the answers and I'll execute. If you're uncertain on any of them, my defaults are: **A = structure templated, content dynamic**; **B = (i) default path**; **C = queue as follow-up**.

input.content:
# Phase 4 Review Template (create-track / create-track-variant)

Canonical presentation format for Phase 4 Generation Review. **Structure is templated, content is dynamic.** Render this for every Phase 4 review so the user sees a consistent, scannable format.

**Applies to:** `/create-track`, `/create-track-variant`
**Consumer:** Freddie (main conversation, not a sub-agent)
**Referenced from:** `workflow.md` node P4-001 (both skills)

---

## Rendering rules

1. **Always render sections 1–9** in the order below. Do not reorder. Do not skip.
2. **Section 10 "Anomalies"** is conditional — only render if there's something worth flagging (critic timeout, unusual reconciliation, validation warning that was justified).
3. **Section 11 "What to listen for"** is conditional — only render when the user picks `[4] Test in Suno first` in section 12. It is derived per-track via node P4-001a (not stored in this template).
4. **Section 12 "Your choice"** always includes all 5 options. `[4] Test in Suno first` is a default option, not opt-in.
5. **Code blocks for Style / Exclude / Settings / Lyrics** are standalone — each in its own fenced block, ready to copy with a single click. Do not combine them into one block.
6. **Content in braces `{like this}`** is dynamic — fill from Tala's Phase 3 payload.
7. **Emphasis bold/italics** are part of the template — preserve them verbatim.

---

## The template

### 1. Critic Digest

| Critic | Flags | Accepted | Key Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gemini** | {N warnings/infos} | {X}/{N} | {1-line summary of what was flagged and whether it landed} |
| **MiniMax** | {N} | {X}/{N} | {1-line summary} |
| **Grok** | {N} | {X}/{N} | {1-line summary} |

**Accepted: {total-accepted}/{total-flags} · Rejected: {total-rejected}/{total-flags}**

### 2. Notable decisions

Render 1–3 callouts for flags that materially shaped the track. Each callout is a short paragraph (2–4 sentences) explaining the decision and **why**. Use these framings:

- **"The 2/3 majority decision:"** — when two or more critics converged on a change. Name which critics agreed, what they wanted, and whether Tala applied it per the authority rule.
- **"The defensible minority rejection:"** — when Tala rejected a single-voice flag with a craft reason. Name the critic, the flag, and the reasoning Tala used to reject.
- **"The {adjective} push:"** — when a flag aligned with an explicit user framing (e.g., "experimental fusion", "cinematic scale", "raw demo energy") and was accepted even as a minority. Name the critic, the concrete change, and the user framing it honored.

Only render callouts that actually happened. If only one critic flagged anything, only render one callout. Do not manufacture callouts for flags that had no meaningful decision.

### 3. Final Style block

```
{final style block}
```

*{charcount} chars · {element count} elements*

### 4. Exclude Style

```
{final exclude style}
```

*{rationale tag: "minimal default" or "aggressive per user approval — both anchor tags fragile" or similar}*

### 5. Advanced Settings

- **Weirdness: {X}%** — {1-sentence justification from Phase 3}
- **Style Influence: {Y}%** — {1-sentence justification from Phase 3}

### 6. Lyrics

```
{final lyrics block}
```

**Structural notes:** {1–2 sentences explaining the section plan and any intentional structural choices — e.g., "Intro deliberately withholds shamisen", "Bridge is the emotional pivot", "Chorus uses call-and-response between X and Y". This is where the craft reasoning lives.}

### 7. Track Names (all 18 — your choice)

| # | Name | Model | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | {name} | {model} | {strategy} |
| 2 | {name} | {model} | {strategy} |
| ... all 18 rows ... |

Mark Tala's advisory picks with ⭐ (max 3). Include her reasoning in a 1-line footnote below the table:

⭐ = Tala's advisory picks (not curation — all 18 are saved regardless). Her reasoning: "{Tala's quoted reason for the picks}"

### 8. Gates passed

- **charcount**: {X}/{limit} ✓
- **validate-prompt**: {N} errors, {M} warnings ({list any justified warnings with 1-line justification inline})
- **validate-envelope**: {PASS / N/A for non-BGM}
- **tri-critic**: All 3 ran, no skips

### 9. Cost summary

{1 line per model that ran a critic call: `Model (N calls): $X.XXXX — in/out tokens`}

**Total: ~${total cost}** {add "+ tracknames internal calls (not surfaced)" if applicable}

### 10. Anomalies (conditional)

Only render if something non-standard happened this run. Examples:
- Critic timeouts + retries
- Tool failures that were worked around
- Unusual reconciliation conflicts that required custom handling
- First-time sound formulas with no prior Tala knowledge

If nothing noteworthy, omit this section entirely. Do not render a "No anomalies" stub.

### 11. What to listen for (conditional — only when user picks [4])

Derived per-track via node P4-001a. Render as a numbered list of 4–6 specific, binary, testable items. Each item must:

- Reference a concrete decision from Phase 2 or Phase 3
- Be checkable by listening once (no spectrum analyzer, no DAW inspection)
- Point at something that could plausibly fail (not "is it music?")
- Name the specific critic-accepted flag or structural decision being tested

Example shape (content will differ per track):

> 1. **Does the shamisen actually enter after the intro?** The intro deliberately withholds it. If Suno puts shamisen in bar 1, the unveil is broken.
> 2. **Is the shamisen leading, or comping?** Role clash check — "strict lead" / "strict comping" language is supposed to fix it.
> 3. **Does the call-and-response land in the chorus?** This is where the fusion earns its keep.
> 4. **Does it drift into smooth jazz / tropical / any vocals?** Exclude stress test.
> 5. **Does the bossa groove feel at 78 BPM, or does Suno push it to 82-85?** BPM adherence is a known soft spot.

If the track has fewer than 4 legitimate items, render what's real. Quality over count.

### 12. Your choice

Always render all 5 options. Do not conditionally hide any.

```
[1] Approve              → Phase 5 writes the file and logs the build
[2] Adjust               → tell me what to change (re-dispatch Tala with notes)
[3] Retry                → fresh Phase 3 generation (same locked analysis)
[4] Test in Suno first   → I'll hold here, give you paste-ready blocks and a listening checklist
[5] Cancel               → abort
```

---

## Test-in-Suno sub-flow (rendered when user picks [4])

When the user picks `[4]`, render this follow-up block. Do **not** re-render sections 1–10; the user has already seen them. Consolidate everything the user needs to copy and verify into one scrollable block.

### Paste-ready blocks

Re-render **Style** / **Exclude Style** / **Advanced Settings** / **Lyrics** as standalone code blocks (one fenced block per field, labeled with a `###` header). The user will copy each one into Suno's corresponding field. These are the SAME blocks as sections 3–6 — re-rendering here saves the user from scrolling back up.

### What to listen for

Render section 11 here (the derived per-track checklist). This is the primary value of the test-in-Suno sub-flow — it tells the user what to verify specifically.

### When you come back

Render this options block verbatim:

```
Tell me what you heard. Options:

- "Sounds great"        → Phase 5 saves the file + you pick a track name (default: Tala's top advisory ⭐ pick)
- "Sounds X but Y"      → tell me what's off and I'll adjust (re-dispatch Tala with notes)
- "Didn't work at all"  → retry from scratch, fresh Phase 3
- "Tweak X only"        → adjust a single element (W/SI, BPM, one instrument) without a full retry

Take your time. I'll be here.
```

Then **stop** and wait for the user to respond. Do not proceed until they come back with feedback.

---

## Rendering examples

### Section 2 — example callouts

**For a track where 2 of 3 critics agreed on a genre reframe:**

> **The 2/3 majority decision:** Grok + MiniMax converged on reframing the genre label from `bossa nova shamisen fusion` → `shamisen-led bossa jazz fusion`. Lead-instrument-first anchors better than dual-culture stacking, which triggers Suno genre-gravity on both sides. **Accepted per authority rule.**

**For a track where Tala rejected a single-voice craft flag:**

> **The defensible minority rejection:** Gemini wanted `[Shamisen lead | Nylon comping | Walking bass]` stripped from Style entirely. Tala rejected — argued this is the only instrument-level signal in the Style block after the genre label, and envelope validator confirms structure is clean. Tala's call. Minority rejection, defensible on craft.

**For a track where a minority flag aligned with user framing:**

> **The experimental-fusion push (accepted, aligns with your framing):** Grok flagged that "sparse lead phrases" was too abstract. Tala added concrete technique — **bent single-note phrases syncopated to bossa clave, open-string ring, major-7th voicings** in the Chorus. That's the fusion earning its keep.

### Section 11 — example listening checklist (one-off, will differ per track)

For reference: the Silk and Breeze build derived this 5-item checklist from Phase 2 health-check cautions, Phase 3 structural choices, and the Exclude Style's top drift vectors. Every item pointed at a specific decision that could plausibly fail in Suno's generation:

1. **Does the shamisen actually enter after the intro?** (intro-unveil decision)
2. **Is the shamisen leading, or comping?** (role-clash fix test)
3. **Does the call-and-response land in the chorus?** (fusion-push flag verification)
4. **Does it drift into smooth jazz / tropical / koto / any vocals?** (exclude stress test)
5. **Does the bossa groove feel at 78 BPM, or does Suno push it to 82-85?** (BPM adherence — known Suno soft spot)

None of these would apply to a different track. The structure (4–6 items, specific, binary, testable) is the template. The content is always track-specific.

---

## Version history

- **2026-04-09** — initial version. Locked in after the Silk and Breeze build (`vault/studio/tracks/2026-04-09/180025-silk-and-breeze.md`) proved this format worked for phase-gated review. User feedback explicitly approved the iteration pattern.

Re-render **Style** / **Exclude Style** / **Advanced Settings** / **Lyrics** as standalone code blocks (one fenced block per field, labeled with a `###` header). The user will copy each one into Suno's corresponding field. These are the SAME blocks as sections 3–6 — re-rendering here saves the user from scrolling back up.

### What to listen for

Render section 11 here (the derived per-track checklist). This is the primary value of the test-in-Suno sub-flow — it tells the user what to verify specifically.

### When you come back

Render this options block verbatim:

```
Tell me what you heard. Options:

- "Sounds great"        → Phase 5 saves the file + you pick a track name (default: Tala's top advisory ⭐ pick)
- "Sounds X but Y"      → tell me what's off and I'll adjust (re-dispatch Tala with notes)
- "Didn't work at all"  → retry from scratch, fresh Phase 3
- "Tweak X only"        → adjust a single element (W/SI, BPM, one instrument) without a full retry

Take your time. I'll be here.
```

Then **stop** and wait for the user to respond. Do not proceed until they come back with feedback.

---

## Rendering examples

### Section 2 — example callouts

**For a track where 2 of 3 critics agreed on a genre reframe:**

> **The 2/3 majority decision:** Grok + MiniMax converged on reframing the genre label from `bossa nova shamisen fusion` → `shamisen-led bossa jazz fusion`. Lead-instrument-first anchors better than dual-culture stacking, which triggers Suno genre-gravity on both sides. **Accepted per authority rule.**

**For a track where Tala rejected a single-voice craft flag:**

> **The defensible minority rejection:** Gemini wanted `[Shamisen lead | Nylon comping | Walking bass]` stripped from Style entirely. Tala rejected — argued this is the only instrument-level signal in the Style block after the genre label, and envelope validator confirms structure is clean. Tala's call. Minority rejection, defensible on craft.

**For a track where a minority flag aligned with user framing:**

> **The experimental-fusion push (accepted, aligns with your framing):** Grok flagged that "sparse lead phrases" was too abstract. Tala added concrete technique — **bent single-note phrases syncopated to bossa clave, open-string ring, major-7th voicings** in the Chorus. That's the fusion earning its keep.

### Section 11 — example listening checklist (one-off, will differ per track)

For reference: the Silk and Breeze build derived this 5-item checklist from Phase 2 health-check cautions, Phase 3 structural choices, and the Exclude Style's top drift vectors. Every item pointed at a specific decision that could plausibly fail in Suno's generation:

1. **Does the shamisen actually enter after the intro?** (intro-unveil decision)
2. **Is the shamisen leading, or comping?** (role-clash fix test)
3. **Does the call-and-response land in the chorus?** (fusion-push flag verification)
4. **Does it drift into smooth jazz / tropical / koto / any vocals?** (exclude stress test)
5. **Does the bossa groove feel at 78 BPM, or does Suno push it to 82-85?** (BPM adherence — known Suno soft spot)

None of these would apply to a different track. The structure (4–6 items, specific, binary, testable) is the template. The content is always track-specific.

---

## Version history

- **2026-04-09** — initial version. Locked in after the Silk and Breeze build (`vault/studio/tracks/2026-04-09/180025-silk-and-breeze.md`) proved this format worked for phase-gated review. User feedback explicitly approved the iteration pattern.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so m…
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep…
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/…
- claude-api: Build Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v1/agents`, `/v1/sessions`); or asks to add a Claude feature (prompt caching, ad…
- architect: Design a technical architecture — McCall analyzes the PRD, dispatches council for multi-model analysis, and produces an architecture doc with decisions, trade-offs, and component design
- brain: Manage brain.db — sync memory/reference files, search, query decisions, or show stats. Use for manual sync, lookup, or diagnostics.
- bye: End a session — reflect, sync brain.db, log to daily
- code-review: Review local code for architecture alignment, quality gaps, and correctness — McCall analyzes with council, Echo provides second opinion, then fix/task/cancel
- create-brief: Create a problem brief — Ryan analyzes the problem, queries brain.db for context, and produces a structured brief with goals, constraints, risks, and success criteria
- create-music-card: Create a music card from a YouTube URL — phase-gated extraction with user confirmation, audio analysis via librosa, Gemini refinement with user context, Obsidian reference card with Suno/MiniMax translations.
- create-prd: Create a PRD from a brief — Ryan expands a problem brief into a full product requirements document with user stories, acceptance criteria, and priorities
- create-skill: Scaffold a new Claude Code skill directory — collects name, description, and tier, then generates valid SKILL.md and workflow skeleton files
- create-task: Create task docs from architecture — McCall slices an architecture doc into scoped task docs for Ryan, with acceptance criteria, file scope, and execution order
- create-track: Create a Suno AI track — single track per run with phase-gated analysis, generation, tri-critic validation, and user review
- create-track-variant: Create a variant of an existing Suno track — loads source Style/Lyrics as starting point, applies modifier, same phase-gated flow
- daily: Create or update a daily note — append tasks, meetings, or notes. Supports date prefix. Empty args checks/creates today's note.
- dev-end: End a dev session — confirm task status, update Penny (done or paused), save checkpoint, reflect, snapshot docs to vault, log to daily
- dev-start: Start a dev session — list tasks, pick one or start new, mark in-progress, load context from brain.db and project docs, dispatch McCall for analysis and Ryan for execution
- gemini: Gemini CLI proxy — research genres, critique Style blocks/lyrics, or analyze mix balance via a second AI perspective
- lyrics: Write lyrics or instrumental structure tags for Suno AI or MiniMax Music — single variation with tri-critic validation and lyrics-analyze quality gate
- meeting: Create a meeting note linked to the daily note. Supports date, time, attendees, and agenda.
- minimax-art: Generate album art, covers, and visual content via MiniMax image-01 API. Translates sonic concepts into images with Obsidian-native indexed folders.
- minimax-music: Generate MiniMax Music prompts — vocal-forward tracks, mood-based generation, 6-section analysis, lo-fi with vocal textures, production
- minimax-video: Generate music videos and visual narratives via MiniMax Hailuo API. Supports first-frame chaining with Iris art, rerolls, and Obsidian-native indexed folders.
- morning: Boot up Freddie — run the session briefing with tasks, inbox status, and suggestions. Start every session with this.
- penny: Obsidian vault manager — daily notes, meetings, tasks, weekly summaries, search, timezone
- pr-review: Review a pull request — fetch PR diff via gh, McCall analyzes with council, Echo provides second opinion, writes summary to out/
- research: Research a topic via NotebookLM — create notebook, add sources from web search, query with specific questions, save findings to project
- save-raw: [DEPRECATED] Raw context is now captured automatically via hooks
- security-audit: Security audit on local code — McCall scans for OWASP top 10, secrets, auth gaps, dependency risks, then fix/task/cancel
- tracknames: Generate emotionally resonant track/song names from a Style block, lyrics, and mood
- weekly: Generate a weekly summary from daily notes and their linked docs. Week runs Monday–Sunday.
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- claude-mem:do: Execute a phased implementation plan using subagents. Use when asked to execute, run, or carry out a plan — especially one created by make-plan.
- claude-mem:smart-explore: Token-optimized structural code search using tree-sitter AST parsing. Use instead of reading full files when you need to understand code structure, find functions, or explore a codebase efficiently.
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- claude-mem:timeline-report: Generate a "Journey Into [Project]" narrative report analyzing a project's entire development history from claude-mem's timeline. Use when asked for a timeline report, project history analysis, development journey, or full project report.
- discord:access: Manage Discord channel access — approve pairings, edit allowlists, set DM/group policy. Use when the user asks to pair, approve someone, check who's allowed, or change policy for the Discord channel.
- discord:configure: Set up the Discord channel — save the bot token and review access policy. Use when the user pastes a Discord bot token, asks to configure Discord, asks "how do I set this up" or "who can reach me," or wants to check channel status.

## [P4-001] Format generation output (diff review)

> **Runs in:** Freddie (main conversation)

**Input:** Tala's reconciliation payload from Phase 3 (ORIGINAL + MODIFIED + auto_fix_table + unresolved_conflicts)
**Output:** Formatted diff review presented to user for Phase 4 decision

Present in this order:

**1. Auto-fix table** — transparency about what was mechanically auto-applied and by whom. Required even if the table is empty (show "No compliance changes" in that case).

| Source | Target | Change | Rationale |
|--------|--------|--------|-----------|
| (from auto-fix-table.json — attribute each row to its critic source) |

**2. Diff review** — show ORIGINAL vs MODIFIED side by side, or sequentially with clear separators if side-by-side is too wide. Highlight what changed using `---` / `+++` markers or show both versions clearly labeled.

```
── ORIGINAL (post-compliance baseline) ──
### Style
{style block}

### Lyrics
{lyrics block}

── MODIFIED (quality flags applied) ──
### Style
{style block — with changes marked}

### Lyrics
{lyrics block — with changes marked}
```

If ORIGINAL === MODIFIED (no quality flags applied, or all quality flags conflicted), show only ORIGINAL and note "No quality changes applied."

**3. Unresolved conflicts** — if any, list each with both candidates and a prompt for user to pick one. Format:

```
Conflict N: target={target}
  {source_a} suggests: "{candidate_a.text}"
  {source_b} suggests: "{candidate_b.text}"
  Your call: [A] {source_a} [B] {source_b} [C] Neither (keep ORIGINAL)
```

If there are no unresolved conflicts, skip this section.

**4. Track summary table** — final ORIGINAL values (genre, BPM, key, instruments, W/SI).

**5. Track names table** — all 18 with model tags + strategy labels.

**6. Production notes + cost summary** — char count, element count, frequency map, Gemini/MiniMax/Grok tokens + cost.

**Note:** If unresolved conflicts exist, Freddie MUST resolve each one before allowing the user to proceed to the ORIGINAL/MODIFIED pick. Ask about conflicts first, collect all answers, then proceed to P4-002.

**Next:** → P4-002
input.new_string:
## [P4-001] Format generation output

> **Runs in:** Freddie (main conversation)

**Input:** Tala's reconciliation payload from Phase 3 (ORIGINAL + MODIFIED + auto_fix_table + unresolved_conflicts)
**Output:** Formatted Phase 4 review presented to user for decision

**Render using the canonical template at `assets/phase4-review-template.md`.** That file is the single source of truth for the Phase 4 presentation format — do not improvise a different layout. Read it before rendering if you're uncertain about any section.

### What the template enforces

- **Structure is templated, content is dynamic.** The sections, order, and framing are fixed. The values inside them come from Tala's Phase 3 payload.
- **12 mandatory sections** rendered in order: Critic Digest → Notable decisions → Style → Exclude → Advanced Settings → Lyrics → Track Names → Gates → Cost → (optional) Anomalies → (conditional) What to listen for → Your choice.
- **Paste-ready code blocks.** Style / Exclude / Settings / Lyrics each live in their own standalone fenced block. Do not combine them.
- **Callouts are earned.** Render "2/3 majority decision", "defensible minority rejection", or "{adjective} push" callouts only when they actually happened. Do not manufacture them.
- **"Test in Suno first" is a default option** in section 12, not opt-in. See P4-002 for the five-option set.

### Conflict handling

If Tala's reconciliation payload contains unresolved conflicts (two critics proposing different changes to the same target with no clear winner), Freddie MUST resolve each one **before** presenting the full Phase 4 review. Format each conflict as:

```
Conflict N: target={target}
  {source_a} suggests: "{candidate_a.text}"
  {source_b} suggests: "{candidate_b.text}"
  Your call: [A] {source_a}  [B] {source_b}  [C] Neither (keep ORIGINAL)
```

Collect all conflict answers, apply the selected candidates to produce a final MODIFIED, then proceed to render the Phase 4 review template with the resolved version.

### If ORIGINAL === MODIFIED

When Tala applied no quality changes (all flags rejected, or all flags conflicted), render the template as normal — sections 3–6 will just show the ORIGINAL content, and the Critic Digest will show `0/N accepted`. Add a 1-line note after section 2: *"No quality changes applied — rendering post-compliance baseline."*

**Next:** → P4-001a (derive listening checklist, held in reserve) → P4-002 (handle user choice)

---

## [P4-001a] Derive "What to listen for" checklist

> **Runs in:** Freddie (main conversation)

**Input:** Phase 2 analysis, Phase 3 reconciliation payload, final Style/Exclude/Lyrics
**Output:** 4–6 numbered, track-specific, binary listening items held in reserve — rendered only when the user picks `[4] Test in Suno first` in P4-002

This checklist is the primary value of the test-in-Suno sub-flow. It tells the user **exactly what to verify** in the Suno output, derived from the decisions that could plausibly fail in generation.

### Sources to derive from

1. **Phase 2 CAUTION flags** — genre gravity warnings, weak-tag callouts, skipped-card costs, health-check cautions
2. **Phase 3 structural decisions** — deliberate unveils, call-and-response placements, role-clash fixes, section transitions
3. **Critic-accepted flags that shaped the track** — especially fusion-pushes, technique-specific additions, language tightenings
4. **Exclude Style top drift vectors** — the 2–3 biggest risks the exclude is guarding against
5. **BPM/key adherence** — always a valid item; Suno is known to drift here on weak-tag genres

### Rules

- **Every item must reference a concrete decision** from Phase 2 or 3. No generic items ("does it sound good", "is the mix clean").
- **Every item must be binary and testable by listening once.** No spectrum analyzer, no DAW inspection, no side-by-side comparison.
- **Every item must point at something that could plausibly fail.** "Is it music?" is not a valid item.
- **4–6 items is the target range.** If the track has fewer than 4 legitimate items, render what's real. Quality over count.
- **Name the specific critic flag or structural decision being tested** in each item, so the user understands why that's the thing to listen for.

### Output shape

```
1. **{Binary question about a specific decision}?** {1-sentence reminder of the decision and what failure looks like.}
2. **{Binary question}?** {1-sentence context.}
...
```

### When to render

**Do not render this checklist in the initial P4-001 review.** It is held in reserve and rendered only when the user picks `[4] Test in Suno first` in P4-002. The test-in-Suno sub-flow in the template consumes it.

**Next:** → P4-002

-If ORIGINAL === MODIFIED (no quality flags applied, or all quality flags conflicted), show only ORIGINAL and note "No quality changes applied."
+**Next:** → P4-001a (derive listening checklist, held in reserve) → P4-002 (handle user choice)
-**3. Unresolved conflicts** — if any, list each with both candidates and a prompt for user to pick one. Format:
-If there are no unresolved conflicts, skip this section.
-**4. Track summary table** — final ORIGINAL values (genre, BPM, key, instruments, W/SI).
+**Input:** Phase 2 analysis, Phase 3 reconciliation payload, final Style/Exclude/Lyrics
+**Output:** 4–6 numbered, track-specific, binary listening items held in reserve — rendered only when the user picks `[4] Test in Suno first` in P4-002
-**5. Track names table** — all 18 with model tags + strategy labels.
+This checklist is the primary value of the test-in-Suno sub-flow. It tells the user **exactly what to verify** in the Suno output, derived from the decisions that could plausibly fail in generation.
-**6. Production notes + cost summary** — char count, element count, frequency map, Gemini/MiniMax/Grok tokens + cost.

-**Note:** If unresolved conflicts exist, Freddie MUST resolve each one before allowing the user to proceed to the ORIGINAL/MODIFIED pick. Ask about conflicts first, collect all answers, then proceed to P4-002.
+1. **Phase 2 CAUTION flags** — genre gravity warnings, weak-tag callouts, skipped-card costs, health-check cautions
+2. **Phase 3 structural decisions** — deliberate unveils, call-and-response placements, role-clash fixes, section transitions
+3. **Critic-accepted flags that shaped the track** — especially fusion-pushes, technique-specific additions, language tightenings
+4. **Exclude Style top drift vectors** — the 2–3 biggest risks the exclude is guarding against
+5. **BPM/key adherence** — always a valid item; Suno is known to drift here on weak-tag genres
+- **Every item must reference a concrete decision** from Phase 2 or 3. No generic items ("does it sound good", "is the mix clean").
+- **Every item must be binary and testable by listening once.** No spectrum analyzer, no DAW inspection, no side-by-side comparison.
+- **Every item must point at something that could plausibly fail.** "Is it music?" is not a valid item.
+- **4–6 items is the target range.** If the track has fewer than 4 legitimate items, render what's real. Quality over count.
+- **Name the specific critic flag or structural decision being tested** in each item, so the user understands why that's the thing to listen for.
+1. **{Binary question about a specific decision}?** {1-sentence reminder of the decision and what failure looks like.}

+**Do not render this checklist in the initial P4-001 review.** It is held in reserve and rendered only when the user picks `[4] Test in Suno first` in P4-002. The test-in-Suno sub-flow in the template consumes it.

## [P4-002] Handle user choice

> **Runs in:** Freddie (main conversation)

**If unresolved conflicts were present in P4-001**, Freddie MUST collect conflict resolutions first (before showing options below). Ask about each conflict, wait for user answers ([A]/[B]/[C] per conflict), apply the selected candidates to a final version, then proceed.

Present options:
```
[1] Use ORIGINAL  — ship the post-compliance baseline, skip quality changes
[2] Use MODIFIED  — ship with all quality changes applied
[3] Adjust        — pick a base (O or M), add notes, re-dispatch Tala
[4] Re-run        — fresh Phase 3 with locked analysis
[5] Cancel        — abort
```

| Choice | Action |
|--------|--------|
| `[1] Use ORIGINAL` | Proceed to Phase 5 with the ORIGINAL (post-compliance baseline) as the final version |
| `[2] Use MODIFIED` | Proceed to Phase 5 with the MODIFIED (quality flags applied) as the final version |
| `[3] Adjust` | Ask: "Which base? [O] ORIGINAL or [M] MODIFIED?" — collect base choice + free-form notes — re-dispatch Tala with locked analysis + base choice + notes — Tala regenerates Phase 3 from chosen base — return to P4-001 |
| `[4] Re-run` | Re-dispatch Tala with locked Phase 2 analysis (fresh Phase 3 generation). Return to P4-001 |
| `[5] Cancel` | Abort |

**Note:** There is no "Mix" or "Test in Suno" option. These were explicitly removed.
input.new_string:
## [P4-002] Handle user choice

> **Runs in:** Freddie (main conversation)

**If unresolved conflicts were present in P4-001**, Freddie MUST collect conflict resolutions first (before showing options below). Ask about each conflict, wait for user answers ([A]/[B]/[C] per conflict), apply the selected candidates to a final version, then proceed.

Present the canonical five-option set (also defined in `assets/phase4-review-template.md` section 12):

```
[1] Approve              → Phase 5 writes the file and logs the build
[2] Adjust               → tell me what to change (re-dispatch Tala with notes)
[3] Retry                → fresh Phase 3 generation (same locked analysis)
[4] Test in Suno first   → I'll hold here, give you paste-ready blocks and a listening checklist
[5] Cancel               → abort
```

| Choice | Action |
|--------|--------|
| `[1] Approve` | Proceed to Phase 5 with Tala's final version (MODIFIED if quality changes were applied, otherwise ORIGINAL). |
| `[2] Adjust` | Collect free-form user notes. Re-dispatch Tala with locked analysis + base version + notes. Tala regenerates Phase 3 from that base. Return to P4-001. |
| `[3] Retry` | Re-dispatch Tala with locked Phase 2 analysis (fresh Phase 3 generation, no notes). Return to P4-001. |
| `[4] Test in Suno first` | Do NOT re-render sections 1–10 of the Phase 4 review. Render the test-in-Suno sub-flow from `assets/phase4-review-template.md` (paste-ready blocks + listening checklist from P4-001a + "when you come back" options). Then **stop and wait** for the user to report back. When they do, map their response to one of: Approve → Phase 5; Adjust → re-dispatch Tala with their notes; Retry → fresh Phase 3; Tweak-only → targeted single-element re-dispatch. |
| `[5] Cancel` | Abort the workflow. Do not write to disk. Do not log decisions. |

### Test-in-Suno return handling

When the user comes back from testing in Suno, Freddie classifies their response before acting:

| User says | Maps to |
|---|---|
| "Sounds great", "approved", "this works", "perfect" | `[1] Approve` — go to Phase 5 |
| "X but Y", "mostly good except", free-form critique | `[2] Adjust` — re-dispatch Tala with their notes as the adjustment |
| "Didn't work at all", "nothing was right", "trash" | `[3] Retry` — fresh Phase 3 |
| "Tweak only X" (single field), "just bump W to 40", "BPM should be 75" | Targeted single-element re-dispatch — Tala regenerates only the named field, not the whole track |

If the user's response is ambiguous, ask a clarifying question before re-dispatching Tala. Do not guess which path to take on unclear feedback.

### Why "Test in Suno first" exists

Suno generation is the ultimate source of truth for whether a track works. Tri-critic catches structural and craft issues, but it cannot predict how Suno will interpret a specific tag combination. The test-in-Suno path lets the user validate against real output before committing the track to disk — saving reroll cycles and catching drift the critics missed. This is especially valuable for:

- **Experimental fusions** (weak anchor tags on both sides)
- **First-time sound formulas** with no prior Tala knowledge
- **Tracks where critics disagreed significantly** (low accepted rate)
- **Tracks that skipped music cards** (BPM/key not measured from audio)

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so m…
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep…
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/…
- claude-api: Build Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v1/agents`, `/v1/sessions`); or asks to add a Claude feature (prompt caching, ad…
- architect: Design a technical architecture — McCall analyzes the PRD, dispatches council for multi-model analysis, and produces an architecture doc with decisions, trade-offs, and component design
- brain: Manage brain.db — sync memory/reference files, search, query decisions, or show stats. Use for manual sync, lookup, or diagnostics.
- bye: End a session — reflect, sync brain.db, log to daily
- code-review: Review local code for architecture alignment, quality gaps, and correctness — McCall analyzes with council, Echo provides second opinion, then fix/task/cancel
- create-brief: Create a problem brief — Ryan analyzes the problem, queries brain.db for context, and produces a structured brief with goals, constraints, risks, and success criteria
- create-music-card: Create a music card from a YouTube URL — phase-gated extraction with user confirmation, audio analysis via librosa, Gemini refinement with user context, Obsidian reference card with Suno/MiniMax translations.
- create-prd: Create a PRD from a brief — Ryan expands a problem brief into a full product requirements document with user stories, acceptance criteria, and priorities
- create-skill: Scaffold a new Claude Code skill directory — collects name, description, and tier, then generates valid SKILL.md and workflow skeleton files
- create-task: Create task docs from architecture — McCall slices an architecture doc into scoped task docs for Ryan, with acceptance criteria, file scope, and execution order
- create-track: Create a Suno AI track — single track per run with phase-gated analysis, generation, tri-critic validation, and user review
- create-track-variant: Create a variant of an existing Suno track — loads source Style/Lyrics as starting point, applies modifier, same phase-gated flow
- daily: Create or update a daily note — append tasks, meetings, or notes. Supports date prefix. Empty args checks/creates today's note.
- dev-end: End a dev session — confirm task status, update Penny (done or paused), save checkpoint, reflect, snapshot docs to vault, log to daily
- dev-start: Start a dev session — list tasks, pick one or start new, mark in-progress, load context from brain.db and project docs, dispatch McCall for analysis and Ryan for execution
- gemini: Gemini CLI proxy — research genres, critique Style blocks/lyrics, or analyze mix balance via a second AI perspective
- lyrics: Write lyrics or instrumental structure tags for Suno AI or MiniMax Music — single variation with tri-critic validation and lyrics-analyze quality gate
- meeting: Create a meeting note linked to the daily note. Supports date, time, attendees, and agenda.
- minimax-art: Generate album art, covers, and visual content via MiniMax image-01 API. Translates sonic concepts into images with Obsidian-native indexed folders.
- minimax-music: Generate MiniMax Music prompts — vocal-forward tracks, mood-based generation, 6-section analysis, lo-fi with vocal textures, production
- minimax-video: Generate music videos and visual narratives via MiniMax Hailuo API. Supports first-frame chaining with Iris art, rerolls, and Obsidian-native indexed folders.
- morning: Boot up Freddie — run the session briefing with tasks, inbox status, and suggestions. Start every session with this.
- penny: Obsidian vault manager — daily notes, meetings, tasks, weekly summaries, search, timezone
- pr-review: Review a pull request — fetch PR diff via gh, McCall analyzes with council, Echo provides second opinion, writes summary to out/
- research: Research a topic via NotebookLM — create notebook, add sources from web search, query with specific questions, save findings to project
- save-raw: [DEPRECATED] Raw context is now captured automatically via hooks
- security-audit: Security audit on local code — McCall scans for OWASP top 10, secrets, auth gaps, dependency risks, then fix/task/cancel
- tracknames: Generate emotionally resonant track/song names from a Style block, lyrics, and mood
- weekly: Generate a weekly summary from daily notes and their linked docs. Week runs Monday–Sunday.
- claude-mem:do: Execute a phased implementation plan using subagents. Use when asked to execute, run, or carry out a plan — especially one created by make-plan.
- claude-mem:smart-explore: Token-optimized structural code search using tree-sitter AST parsing. Use instead of reading full files when you need to understand code structure, find functions, or explore a codebase efficiently.
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- claude-mem:timeline-report: Generate a "Journey Into [Project]" narrative report analyzing a project's entire development history from claude-mem's timeline. Use when asked for a timeline report, project history analysis, development journey, or full project report.
- discord:configure: Set up the Discord channel — save the bot token and review access policy. Use when the user pastes a Discord bot token, asks to configure Discord, asks "how do I set this up" or "who can reach me," or wants to check channel status.
- discord:access: Manage Discord channel access — approve pairings, edit allowlists, set DM/group policy. Use when the user asks to pair, approve someone, check who's allowed, or change policy for the Discord channel.

- meeting: Create a meeting note linked to the daily note. Supports date, time, attendees, and agenda.
- minimax-art: Generate album art, covers, and visual content via MiniMax image-01 API. Translates sonic concepts into images with Obsidian-native indexed folders.
- minimax-music: Generate MiniMax Music prompts — vocal-forward tracks, mood-based generation, 6-section analysis, lo-fi with vocal textures, production
- minimax-video: Generate music videos and visual narratives via MiniMax Hailuo API. Supports first-frame chaining with Iris art, rerolls, and Obsidian-native indexed folders.
- morning: Boot up Freddie — run the session briefing with tasks, inbox status, and suggestions. Start every session with this.
- penny: Obsidian vault manager — daily notes, meetings, tasks, weekly summaries, search, timezone
- pr-review: Review a pull request — fetch PR diff via gh, McCall analyzes with council, Echo provides second opinion, writes summary to out/
- research: Research a topic via NotebookLM — create notebook, add sources from web search, query with specific questions, save findings to project
- save-raw: [DEPRECATED] Raw context is now captured automatically via hooks
- security-audit: Security audit on local code — McCall scans for OWASP top 10, secrets, auth gaps, dependency risks, then fix/task/cancel
- tracknames: Generate emotionally resonant track/song names from a Style block, lyrics, and mood
- weekly: Generate a weekly summary from daily notes and their linked docs. Week runs Monday–Sunday.
- claude-mem:do: Execute a phased implementation plan using subagents. Use when asked to execute, run, or carry out a plan — especially one created by make-plan.
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- claude-mem:timeline-report: Generate a "Journey Into [Project]" narrative report analyzing a project's entire development history from claude-mem's timeline. Use when asked for a timeline report, project history analysis, development journey, or full project report.
- claude-mem:smart-explore: Token-optimized structural code search using tree-sitter AST parsing. Use instead of reading full files when you need to understand code structure, find functions, or explore a codebase efficiently.
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- discord:configure: Set up the Discord channel — save the bot token and review access policy. Use when the user pastes a Discord bot token, asks to configure Discord, asks "how do I set this up" or "who can reach me," or wants to check channel status.
- discord:access: Manage Discord channel access — approve pairings, edit allowlists, set DM/group policy. Use when the user asks to pair, approve someone, check who's allowed, or change policy for the Discord channel.

- Production notes (char count, element count, frequency map, slot validation)

## Phase 4 — Generation Review

Present results in this order:

**1. Critic Digest Table:**
```
| Critic  | Flags | Accepted | Key Changes                          |
|---------|-------|----------|--------------------------------------|
| Gemini  | N     | N/N      | ...                                  |
| MiniMax | N     | N/N      | ...                                  |
| Grok    | N     | N/N      | ...                                  |
```

**2. Per-critic narrative** — 2-3 sentences each: what was flagged, what was accepted, what was rejected and why.

**3. Track Summary Table** — same format as Phase 2, now with final post-critic values.

**4. Generated Output:**

Style:
```
{style block}
```

Exclude Style:
```
{exclude style}
```

Advanced Settings:
- **Weirdness**: X% — {justification}
- **Style Influence**: X% — {justification}

Lyrics:
```
{full lyrics with typed brackets}
```

**5. Track Names Table** — all 18 with model tags + strategy labels:
```
| # | Name | Model | Strategy |
|---|------|-------|----------|
| 1 | {name — this becomes the title + filename} | {model} | {strategy} |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 18 | ... | ... | ... |
```

**6. Production Notes** — char count, element count, frequency map, BPM rationale, exclude rationale, W/SI justification.

**7. Cost Summary** — show API usage from this phase:
```
=== Phase 3 Usage ===
Gemini:  X in / Y out | $0.00 (N calls)
MiniMax: X in / Y out | $0.00 (N calls)
Grok:    X in / Y out | $0.00 (N calls)
Total:   $0.00
```

Ask user:
```
[1] Approve  [2] Adjust  [3] Retry  [4] Cancel
```

- **Approve** → Phase 5
- **Adjust** → collect user modifications, re-run Phase 3 with adjustments
- **Retry** → re-run Phase 3 from scratch (new generation, same locked analysis)
- **Cancel** → abort, done

User may also say "let me test in Suno first" — wait for feedback, then re-present or adjust based on real-world results.

## Phase 5 — Save

1. Run `getdate` to get folderDate and folderTime
2. Use track name #1 as filename slug (lowercase, hyphenated)
3. Write single `.md` file to: `vault/studio/tracks/{folderDate}/{folderTime}-{track-name-slug}.md`
4. Use the template in `assets/track-output-template.md` for file format
5. Run `reflect` for learning capture + brain.db decision logging
6. If reflect returns `consolidationRecommended: true` → run `consolidate-memory --agent tala`

- minimax-video: Generate music videos and visual narratives via MiniMax Hailuo API. Supports first-frame chaining with Iris art, rerolls, and Obsidian-native indexed folders.
- morning: Boot up Freddie — run the session briefing with tasks, inbox status, and suggestions. Start every session with this.

- tracknames: Generate emotionally resonant track/song names from a Style block, lyrics, and mood
- weekly: Generate a weekly summary from daily notes and their linked docs. Week runs Monday–Sunday.

- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- claude-mem:timeline-report: Generate a "Journey Into [Project]" narrative report analyzing a project's entire development history from claude-mem's timeline. Use when asked for a timeline report, project history analysis, development journey, or full project report.
- claude-mem:smart-explore: Token-optimized structural code search using tree-sitter AST parsing. Use instead of reading full files when you need to understand code structure, find functions, or explore a codebase efficiently.
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- discord:configure: Set up the Discord channel — save the bot token and review access policy. Use when the user pastes a Discord bot token, asks to configure Discord, asks "how do I set this up" or "who can reach me," or wants to check channel status.
- discord:access: Manage Discord channel access — approve pairings, edit allowlists, set DM/group policy. Use when the user asks to pair, approve someone, check who's allowed, or change policy for the Discord channel.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: create-track-variant
description: Create a variant of an existing Suno track — loads source Style/Lyrics as starting point, applies modifier, same phase-gated flow
user-invocable: true
argument-hint: "<path-to-track.md> [modifier prompt]"
agent: none
---

Create a variant of an existing Suno AI track:

**$ARGUMENTS**

## Execution Model

Same as `/create-track` (Freddie orchestrates, Tala executes) with an additional Phase 0 that loads the source track. The variant inherits from the source and applies the modifier prompt on top.

| Phase | Runs in | Purpose |
|-------|---------|---------|
| 0 — Load Source | Freddie (main) | Read and parse the source track file |
| 1 — Analysis | Tala sub-agent | Re-analyze with source context + modifier |
| 2 — Analysis Review | Freddie (main) | Present diff table (inherited vs modified) |
| 3 — Generation | Tala sub-agent | Build variant Style/Lyrics with tri-critic |
| 4 — Generation Review | Freddie (main) | Present critic digest + output |
| 5 — Save | Freddie (main) | Write new .md file (never overwrites source) |

## Phase 0 — Load Source

**Executor:** Freddie (main agent)

1. Read the source track `.md` file at the provided path
2. Extract from the file:
   - Frontmatter (genre, mood, bpm, key, type)
   - Analysis table
   - Style block
   - Exclude Style
   - W/SI values
   - Lyrics
3. Validate the file has the expected structure (frontmatter + required sections)
4. If file is missing or invalid, tell the user and abort

**Output:** Parsed source track data ready to pass to Tala.

## Phase 1 — Analysis (with source context)

Dispatch Tala sub-agent with:
- Source track's full analysis, Style, Lyrics, and frontmatter
- Modifier prompt from user (if provided)
- Section count: inherited from source track, or overridden by modifier (e.g., "make it longer" → 7 sections)
- Instructions: identify what the modifier changes vs what carries forward from source

Tala follows the same P1-001 through P1-006 nodes from `create-track/workflow.md` but with source context:
- Start from source analysis, not from scratch
- If modifier is provided: apply it as adjustments (e.g., "make it darker" → adjust mood, maybe key)
- If no modifier: re-analyze the source with fresh eyes (still inherits the core)
- Mark each dimension as "inherited" or "modified" with explanation

**Returns:** Structured analysis with inherited/modified indicators per dimension.

## Phase 2 — Analysis Review

Present the analysis table with an extra column showing what changed:

```
| Dimension   | Value              | Source        | Status    | Explanation                |
|-------------|--------------------|---------------|-----------|----------------------------|
| Genre       | Jazzhop            | inherited     | same      | Carried from source        |
| Mood        | dark, brooding     | modifier      | changed   | User asked for "darker"    |
| Instruments | shamisen, harp     | inherited     | same      | Carried from source        |
| BPM         | 72                 | tala          | changed   | Slower for darker mood     |
| Key         | D Minor            | tala          | changed   | Darker minor key           |
| Music Card  | none               | inherited     | same      | No match                   |
| Archetype   | none               | inherited     | same      | No archetype               |
```

Ask user:
```
[1] Approve  [2] Adjust  [3] Retry  [4] Cancel
```

Same handling as `/create-track` Phase 2.

## Phase 3 — Generation

Same as `/create-track` Phase 3. Tala follows nodes P3-001 through P3-010 from `create-track/workflow.md`.

The locked analysis includes inherited values from source — Tala uses these as the foundation and builds the variant on top.

## Phase 4 — Generation Review

Same as `/create-track` Phase 4. Present critic digest, track summary, generated output, user choice.

## Phase 5 — Save

Same as `/create-track` Phase 5 with these additions:
- **Never overwrites the source file** — variant gets its own timestamp and name
- Add `variant_of` field to frontmatter pointing to the source track path
- Save to: `vault/studio/tracks/{folderDate}/{folderTime}-{variant-name-slug}.md`

## Output

Same single `.md` format as `/create-track`, with the addition of:
- `variant_of` in frontmatter linking back to source
- Analysis table includes "Status" column (inherited/changed)

- minimax-music: Generate MiniMax Music prompts — vocal-forward tracks, mood-based generation, 6-section analysis, lo-fi with vocal textures, production
- minimax-video: Generate music videos and visual narratives via MiniMax Hailuo API. Supports first-frame chaining with Iris art, rerolls, and Obsidian-native indexed folders.
- morning: Boot up Freddie — run the session briefing with tasks, inbox status, and suggestions. Start every session with this.
- penny: Obsidian vault manager — daily notes, meetings, tasks, weekly summaries, search, timezone
- pr-review: Review a pull request — fetch PR diff via gh, McCall analyzes with council, Echo provides second opinion, writes summary to out/
- research: Research a topic via NotebookLM — create notebook, add sources from web search, query with specific questions, save findings to project
- save-raw: [DEPRECATED] Raw context is now captured automatically via hooks
- security-audit: Security audit on local code — McCall scans for OWASP top 10, secrets, auth gaps, dependency risks, then fix/task/cancel
- tracknames: Generate emotionally resonant track/song names from a Style block, lyrics, and mood
- weekly: Generate a weekly summary from daily notes and their linked docs. Week runs Monday–Sunday.
- claude-mem:do: Execute a phased implementation plan using subagents. Use when asked to execute, run, or carry out a plan — especially one created by make-plan.
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- claude-mem:timeline-report: Generate a "Journey Into [Project]" narrative report analyzing a project's entire development history from claude-mem's timeline. Use when asked for a timeline report, project history analysis, development journey, or full project report.
- claude-mem:smart-explore: Token-optimized structural code search using tree-sitter AST parsing. Use instead of reading full files when you need to understand code structure, find functions, or explore a codebase efficiently.
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- discord:configure: Set up the Discord channel — save the bot token and review access policy. Use when the user pastes a Discord bot token, asks to configure Discord, asks "how do I set this up" or "who can reach me," or wants to check channel status.
- discord:access: Manage Discord channel access — approve pairings, edit allowlists, set DM/group policy. Use when the user asks to pair, approve someone, check who's allowed, or change policy for the Discord channel.
