Dead-subdirs warning gone. Remaining `mermaid-exists` warning is **pre-existing** — not something I caused. I'll flag it as a known todo but not fix it now (scope creep).

---

## ✅ Skill lock-in complete

### What shipped

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `create-track/assets/phase4-review-template.md` | **NEW** — canonical 12-section Phase 4 review format, with render rules, conditional sections, test-in-Suno sub-flow spec, and rendering examples |
| `create-track/workflow.md` | Replaced P4-001 (inline format → template reference) · added **P4-001a** (listening checklist derivation rules) · rebuilt P4-002 (restored Test-in-Suno as `[4]`, added return-handling classifier table, removed contradicting note) |
| `create-track/SKILL.md` | Slimmed Phase 4 section (deleted 65 lines of duplicated inline format, replaced with template reference) · updated mermaid to show P4-001a + 5 numbered options · self-loop on `[4] test in Suno first` |
| `create-track-variant/SKILL.md` | Added template reference + 5-option choice block |
| `create-track-variant/assets/README.md` | **NEW** — documents shared asset references, fixes validator dead-subdirs warning |

### Validation

- ✅ `create-track` — PASS, 0 errors, 0 warnings
- ✅ `create-track-variant` — 0 errors, 1 **pre-existing** warning (`structure.mermaid-exists` — variant SKILL.md needs a mermaid diagram, but that's unrelated to this change)

### What's guaranteed moving forward

Every future `/create-track` and `/create-track-variant` Phase 4 will render:

1. Critic Digest table with **accepted/rejected count** row
2. Up to 3 "Notable decisions" callouts (only when they actually happened — no manufactured content)
3. Paste-ready standalone blocks for Style / Exclude / Settings / Lyrics (each in its own fenced block)
4. Track Names table (all 18, with Tala's advisory picks marked)
5. Gates passed checklist
6. Cost summary
7. 5-option user choice including `[4] Test in Suno first` as a **default path**

And when the user picks `[4]`, they'll get:

1. Re-rendered paste-ready blocks (one scrollable spot, no scrolling back)
2. **Track-specific "what to listen for" checklist** (4–6 items, derived from Phase 2 cautions + Phase 3 structural decisions + exclude drift vectors)
3. "When you come back" options mapped to Approve/Adjust/Retry/Tweak-only

### The key discipline the template enforces

**Structure is templated, content is dynamic.** The skeleton never changes — the scan patterns are always in the same place, so you always know where to look. But the content is derived per-track from the real Phase 2/3 payload. No generic "what to listen for" checklists, no cookie-cutter critic summaries.

---

## Pre-existing follow-up noted

`create-track-variant/SKILL.md` needs a mermaid diagram — validator flagged it, not new, worth doing next time we touch the variant skill.

---

**Ready to commit?** This is a self-contained skill improvement — separate commit from the ASL archive.

`[2]` Also push to origin after commit
`[3]` Leave staged, do something else first

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Lock in Phase 4 review template for create-track(-variant)

Codify the iteration format that worked for the 2026-04-09 "Silk and Breeze"
build (bossa nova shamisen fusion, approved first-try in Suno). Fix the
contradiction between SKILL.md (which listed "Test in Suno first" as an
option) and workflow.md (which claimed it was "explicitly removed"). Make
the presentation consistent across all future runs via a canonical template.

New: assets/phase4-review-template.md
- 12 mandatory sections rendered in fixed order (Critic Digest, Notable
  decisions, Style, Exclude, Settings, Lyrics, Track Names, Gates, Cost,
  Anomalies, What to listen for, Your choice)
- Paste-ready standalone code blocks per field (one fenced block each,
  copyable individually — no combining)
- Per-critic narrative with accepted/rejected counts
- Callouts ("2/3 majority decision", "defensible minority rejection",
  "{adjective} push") rendered only when earned, not manufactured
- Test-in-Suno sub-flow: re-rendered paste blocks + listening checklist +
  "when you come back" options mapped to Approve/Adjust/Retry/Tweak-only
- Rendering examples inline so future Freddie knows what good looks like

workflow.md changes:
- P4-001 rewritten to reference the template file (delete 50+ lines of
  inline format that would drift from the template)
- P4-001a added: derive "what to listen for" checklist from Phase 2
  cautions + Phase 3 structural decisions + exclude drift vectors. Rules:
  4-6 items, binary, testable by listening once, every item references a
  concrete decision that could plausibly fail in Suno
- P4-002 rebuilt: 5 options (Approve, Adjust, Retry, Test in Suno first,
  Cancel) with return-handling classifier table that maps user feedback
  strings ("sounds great" / "X but Y" / "didn't work" / "tweak only X")
  to the right re-dispatch path
- Removed the "no test in Suno" contradicting note
- TOC updated

SKILL.md changes:
- Mermaid diagram shows P4-001 → P4-001a → P4-002 with numbered options
- Phase 4 narrative section slimmed (-65 lines) — replaced with template
  reference instead of duplicating the format
- User choice block shows all 5 options always

create-track-variant:
- SKILL.md Phase 4 section references the shared template via relative
  path (../create-track/assets/phase4-review-template.md) — single source
  of truth for both skills
- Added assets/README.md documenting shared asset references and fixing
  validator dead-subdirs warning

Validation:
- create-track: PASS 0 errors 0 warnings
- create-track-variant: 0 errors, 1 pre-existing warning (mermaid-exists,
  unrelated to this change)

Why: the "Silk and Breeze" iteration (in vault, 2026-04-09 180025) proved
this format works. User feedback was explicit — the accepted/rejected
count, the per-critic narrative, the paste-ready blocks, the "what to
listen for" checklist, and the "test first" option combined to make the
Phase 4 review actually useful for decision-making. Template locks it in
so every future run gets the same format instead of depending on whether
Freddie improvises well that day.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main 2>&1 | tail -10

content.text:
This is a significant finding. Let me name exactly what's new here, because the implications reach further than just "Suno has a new feature."

## What you actually discovered

**Suno now exposes its own canonical interpretation of an audio file.** Upload → it tells you, in its own words, what the track IS. That's the closest thing to ground truth we've ever had for "what does Suno actually understand a track as?" We've been reverse-engineering this for months via reference files, craft rules, and tri-critic feedback. Now there's a direct channel.

The fact that the user's regeneration from those auto-extracted values came out **close to the original** is the validator — if Suno's own extraction regenerates something faithful, that format round-trips. Our typed-bracket pipe format may round-trip just as well, or it may not. We don't know yet.

## Concrete format differences (observations, not conclusions)

### Style block — prose, not pipe

| Aspect | Tala's current craft | Suno's extraction |
|---|---|---|
| **Format** | Pipe-separated, typed brackets | Full prose, sentences |
| **Char count** | 75–120 target (Silk and Breeze: 169) | ~620 chars for Yellow |
| **Structure** | `[Instrumental] \| genre \| [Instr1 \| Instr2 \| Instr3] \| mood \| BPM Key \| mix \| [No Vocals]` | Narrative arrangement: intro → verse → chorus arc described sentence by sentence |
| **Tempo/key placement** | Inline token: `78 BPM F Major` | Separate final sentence: "Tempo is 138 BPM in the key of B Major" |
| **Envelope tags** | Mandatory for BGM | Not present (but this is a vocal track, so we can't conclude from this sample) |
| **Instrument description** | Role + register: `Shamisen lead (high-mid)` | Timbre + behavior: "Clean, arpeggiated electric guitar with light chorus and delay" |

Suno's format reads like a session engineer's tracking sheet. Tala's reads like a terse metadata string. Both can work — but if Suno's internal tokenizer prefers prose, we might be leaving quality on the table by optimizing for char count.

### Lyrics block — similar structure, richer brackets

| Aspect | Tala's current | Suno's extraction |
|---|---|---|
| **Section markers** | Canonical (`[Verse]`, `[Chorus]`) | Canonical ✓ same |
| **Bracket content** | Role/frequency: `[Instrument: Shamisen lead \| Nylon comping]` | Action/entry: `[drums enter with steady backbeat]`, `[breathy male vocals, melodic bass guitar enters]` |
| **Inline vocal direction** | Usually in section header | Mid-line: `[falsetto]` appearing inside a lyric line |
| **Performance actions** | Rare | Present: `[click track count-off: one, two, three, go]`, `[vocalist counts: brothers, one, two, three, go]` |

Tala already uses typed brackets in Lyrics — that's confirmed as a valid Suno pattern. But the **content** of those brackets in Suno's own output is **action-based** ("drums enter with backbeat") rather than **role-based** ("Drums strict rhythm"). Action-based brackets describe moments in time; role-based brackets describe persistent assignments. Different semantics.

The mid-line `[falsetto]` marker is especially interesting — that's a vocal direction tied to a specific lyric word/line, not a section-level setting. We don't do that in our current craft.

## What this unlocks (potentially)

1. **Music card pipeline gets a new field.** Right now `/create-music-card` does: yt-dlp → ffmpeg → librosa analysis → Gemini refinement → card file. With this new Suno feature we could add: **upload same audio to Suno → capture Suno's own Style/Lyrics → save as `suno_interpretation` field on the card.** That gives every music card both the measured data (our current analysis) AND Suno's semantic reading. When Tala later uses that card, she can reference either or both.

2. **Craft rule validation becomes empirical.** We can now take any Tala-generated track, upload its Suno render back into Suno's analyzer, and see how Suno describes what we made. Round-trip fidelity becomes a measurable craft metric. If Tala writes "shamisen-led bossa jazz fusion, 78 BPM F Major" and Suno's own readback says "bossa nova with Japanese folk instruments, 82 BPM G Major"... that's a prompt drift measurement. Real one.

3. **A/B testing format approaches.** Take "Silk and Breeze", rebuild the same track intent in Suno-prose format, generate, compare. Does one version round-trip better? Does one produce more consistent results across rerolls? Does one hold BPM better?

4. **Reference library gets smarter.** Suno's extractions from real artist tracks become a corpus. Ten artist songs' worth of Suno-format extractions is a style reference library with actual Suno-model provenance. That's orders of magnitude better than hand-written descriptors.

## What this does NOT mean (yet)

- **Tala's craft rules are not obsolete.** One sample is not a pattern. The pipe format has worked across dozens of tracks. Prose format has one data point.
- **We should not rewrite `/create-track` craft rules today.** That's exactly the kind of premature pivot that led to ASL's bloat. Evidence first, architecture later.
- **We don't know if prose is better, equal, or worse for BGM.** The Jacob Collier sample is vocal rock. Instrumental fusion tracks may behave differently.
- **We don't know the char limit for Suno's Style field in prose mode.** The 75-120 target came from experimentation with pipe format. Suno accepted ~620 chars of prose — we don't know the ceiling.

## Proposed next steps (pick one or queue all)

### Option A — **Minimum viable validation** (20 min, cheap)

Take the Style + Lyrics Suno extracted for the Collier/Yellow audio. Paste them unchanged into a fresh Suno generation. Does it come out the same? If yes, round-trip is confirmed for vocal rock. If it drifts, we've learned something about Suno's extraction-vs-generation asymmetry.

### Option B — **A/B on Silk and Breeze** (1 hour, more expensive but directly relevant)

Manually rewrite "Silk and Breeze" intent in Suno's prose format (prose Style block, action-based Lyrics brackets, mid-line performance markers where applicable). Generate. Compare against the pipe-format version we shipped today. See which holds the shamisen-led bossa intent better. This is the one that tells us whether to change Tala's craft.

### Option C — **Music card pipeline enhancement** (medium task, ~1 day, high leverage)

Add a new step to `/create-music-card`: after the existing librosa + Gemini pipeline, upload the trimmed audio to Suno via its new feature, capture the returned Style + Lyrics, save as a new frontmatter field (`suno_interpretation`) on the card. Benefit: every music card from now on has Suno's own reading alongside our measured data. Requires: figuring out if Suno's upload-audio feature has an API or needs UI scraping (I'd guess UI-only in current release — worth checking).

### Option D — **Corpus building** (ongoing, low effort per session)

Every time you find interesting reference audio, run it through Suno's upload feature and save the Style + Lyrics in a scratch directory. After ~10 samples we'll have enough to see patterns in how Suno describes different genres, arrangements, and vocal types. No code, no commitments — just data collection.

### Option E — **Write a brief** (immediate, scopes the work)

Dispatch Holmes to investigate this formally: "Suno now exposes its interpretation layer via upload-audio. Investigate whether this should reshape Tala's craft rules, music card pipeline, or both. Gather evidence. Recommend experiments." Holmes produces a brief, we decide from there. This is the "do it properly" path.

---

## My honest recommendation

**Start with D (corpus building) and do A (minimum validation) tonight if you have 20 minutes.**

- D is free and low-risk — just save the Suno extractions somewhere. We don't act on them yet, but in two weeks we'll have enough samples to see if there are patterns.
- A is the cheapest way to confirm the finding isn't a one-off. If the Yellow extraction round-trips, we know this works. If it doesn't, we've learned the extraction is lossy and the tool is less useful than it looks.
- **Don't do B, C, or E until A and D have produced evidence.** The ASL session taught us what happens when we commit engineering before the evidence is in.

The specific thing I'd flag to capture in your scratch notes for D:

> **For each sample** — original audio file, Suno's extracted Style (verbatim), Suno's extracted Lyrics (verbatim), whether the round-trip generation was close/drifted/wrong, your subjective rating of the match (1-5), and the genre/mood tag of the source. After 10 samples, patterns will emerge.

And a concrete artifact I'd create tonight to make D frictionless: **a directory at `vault/studio/research/suno-extraction-samples/`** with a template file for each sample. No code, no tools — just a place for you to drop these findings as you encounter them. Want me to scaffold that directory + template now?

content.text:
Yes — read it. A HAR file from your real upload session is exactly the primary source we need. It'll tell us the actual endpoints, auth mechanism, request/response format, and the network flow. Without it I'd be guessing based on web archaeology.

Before I open it, two things worth naming:

**1. HAR files contain secrets.** Auth cookies, bearer tokens, session IDs, sometimes even base64-encoded audio payloads. I'll:
- Read it from the Downloads folder (not copy into the repo)
- Never paste auth values into this conversation
- Never commit the HAR file to git
- Summarize endpoints and shapes without leaking credentials

**2. Approach decision comes after the HAR analysis.** The three options you mentioned have very different tradeoffs:

| Approach | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Curl with exported cookies** | Simplest, single-file tool, no dependencies | Breaks when cookies expire (manual re-export), may hit CSRF walls |
| **Playwright** | Resilient, handles session refresh, can use existing login | Heavy dependency, launches a browser, slower |
| **chrome-devtools-mcp** | Piggybacks on your running session | Extra MCP dependency, tied to a running Chrome, novel |

My default instinct is **curl-with-cookies if the auth is cookie-based and stable** — it's the lightest tool that could possibly work. But I won't commit until the HAR tells me what Suno actually requires.

input.prompt:
You are analyzing a HAR file from a real Suno upload-audio session. The goal is to understand Suno's upload-audio API so we can build a tool that reprouces the flow programmatically.

**HAR file:** `C:/Users/odiem/Downloads/suno.com.har` (~17MB, JSON format)

## CRITICAL: credential handling

The HAR contains real auth cookies, bearer tokens, session IDs, CSRF tokens, and possibly a base64-encoded audio payload. You MUST:

1. **Never echo auth values.** Don't paste cookie values, bearer tokens, CSRF tokens, user IDs, or session IDs into your report. Refer to them generically ("a `sess` cookie exists", "a `Authorization: Bearer <redacted>` header is present").
2. **Never copy the file into the repo.** Read it in place from `C:/Users/odiem/Downloads/`.
3. **Don't dump the audio payload.** If you see a multipart upload with a base64 body, note the field name and approximate size, not the content.
4. **Redact any personally identifying info** — email addresses, real names, account IDs. If you can't tell if something is sensitive, err on the side of redacting.

## What to find

The user uploaded an audio file (`1774630111-jacob-collier-improvises-with.mp3`) to Suno's interface. Suno analyzed it and returned a Style block + Lyrics. I need to understand the network flow that made this happen so we can reproduce it in a CLI tool.

Answer these questions in your report:

### 1. Endpoint discovery
Which HTTP requests were made during the upload-analyze-response cycle? I'm looking for:
- The upload endpoint (POST, probably multipart/form-data with the mp3)
- Any intermediate endpoints (pre-signed URL request, upload token, job creation)
- The polling or webhook endpoint (if analysis is async)
- The final metadata fetch (where the Style and Lyrics come back)

For each, give: HTTP method, URL (with path, redact query params if they contain tokens), a 1-line purpose guess.

### 2. Request shape for the upload
- Content-Type header
- Body structure (multipart fields with names, JSON body with keys, etc.)
- Any hidden/important headers (X-Request-ID, X-CSRF, Accept, User-Agent)

### 3. Response shape for the final metadata call
This is the one we care about most — the response that contains the Style and Lyrics. Give:
- Top-level JSON keys
- Where exactly "Style" lives in the response (path like `data.analysis.style_prompt`)
- Where "Lyrics" lives
- Any other useful fields (tempo, key, duration, confidence, genre guesses)

If the response is not JSON, say so and describe what it is.

### 4. Auth mechanism
- Does Suno use Bearer tokens, session cookies, or both?
- Are tokens in the `Authorization` header, a custom header, or cookies?
- Do any of the requests have a CSRF token? If so, where does it come from (meta tag, cookie, separate endpoint)?
- What cookies are being sent on the protected requests (names only, not values)?
- Is there a token refresh flow visible in the HAR (e.g., `/auth/refresh` or similar)?

### 5. Session state
- Is there a workspace ID, user ID, or account ID embedded in URLs or headers?
- Is there a "session" or "workspace" concept that needs to be bootstrapped before upload?

### 6. Rate limits / retry behavior
- Any 429s, 503s, or retry-with-backoff patterns in the HAR?
- Any response headers suggesting rate limits (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After)?

### 7. Third-party services
- Does the upload go directly to Suno, or is it proxied through AWS S3 / Cloudflare R2 / etc.?
- Are there any external service calls (analytics, error tracking) that we can ignore?

### 8. Is this a documented/public API?
- Does the URL pattern suggest it's a public API (`api.suno.com/v1/...`) or an internal frontend API (`suno.com/api/...`)?
- Any `OpenAPI`, `swagger`, or documentation URLs referenced?

### 9. Minimum viable curl
Based on what you find, sketch the rough shape of a curl command that would reproduce the upload. DO NOT include real auth values — use placeholders like `$SUNO_SESSION_COOKIE`. Something like:

```
curl -X POST 'https://suno.com/api/upload' \
  -H 'Cookie: sess=$SUNO_SESSION_COOKIE' \
  -F 'audio=@/path/to/file.mp3' \
  -F 'analyze=true'
```

If it's more complex (pre-signed S3 URL flow, websocket upgrade, multi-step), describe the steps.

### 10. Feasibility verdict
Based on everything you found, rank the three approach options from easiest to hardest for reproducing this programmatically:

- **(A)** Curl with exported session cookies
- **(B)** Playwright / headless browser automation
- **(C)** chrome-devtools-mcp piggybacking on user's live session

Give a 1-2 sentence reason for the ranking. If there's a serious blocker for any approach (CSRF token regenerated per-request from a meta tag, Cloudflare turnstile, websocket upgrade required), flag it.

## Output format

A structured markdown report with sections 1-10 above. Be thorough but do NOT paste large raw bodies. If a request/response is interesting, summarize its shape; don't include the full JSON.

Under 1500 words. The report should be something I can read and decide "yes, go build this" or "this is harder than I thought, we need a different approach."

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me create a comprehensive report:

## Suno Upload-Audio API Analysis Report

### 1. Endpoint Discovery

The upload-analyze-response cycle consists of these HTTP requests:

| Method | URL | Purpose |
|--------|-----|---------|
| POST | `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/` | Create upload session, get S3 pre-signed URL |
| POST | `https://suno-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com/` | Upload MP3 directly to AWS S3 |
| POST | `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/{id}/upload-finish/` | Notify server upload complete |
| GET | `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/{id}/` | Poll analysis progress (called 5x) |
| POST | `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/{id}/initialize-clip/` | Create clip from upload (returns `clip_id`) |

All requests return 200 or 204 (success).

### 2. Request Shape for Upload

**Initial POST to create upload session:**
- **Content-Type**: `application/json`
- **Body**: `{"extension":"mp3"}`
- **Key headers**: `browser-token: <redacted>`, `origin: https://suno.com`, `referer: https://suno.com/`

**Response**: Returns `id`, `url` (S3 bucket), `fields` (form data for S3), `is_file_uploaded`

**S3 Upload (multipart/form-data):**
- Uses AWS SigV4 pre-signed POST
- Form fields: `Content-Type`, `key`, `AWSAccessKeyId`, `policy`, `signature`, `file` (binary MP3)
- Response: 204 No Content

**Upload-finish POST:**
- **Body**: `{"upload_type":"file_upload","upload_filename":"1774630111-jacob-collier-improvises-with.mp3"}`

### 3. Response Shape for Final Metadata

**Polling GET response (when status = "complete")** returns:
```json
{
  "id": "e9c6d2e0-c3b6-4a4e-92e2-d025282e94d6",
  "status": "complete",
  "s3_id": "bd90d2da-438c-43f7-8e04-8f952209474e",
  "title": "1774630111-jacob-collier-improvises-with",
  "image_url": "https://cdn2.suno.ai/image_{s3_id}.jpeg",
  "has_vocal": true,
  "display_tags": "alternative rock, britpop, male falsetto vocals",
  "inferred_description": "[Long AI-generated description of audio characteristics, tempo, key, production details]"
}
```

**Key locations:**
- **Style**: `display_tags` (comma-separated)
- **Lyrics**: Not returned in upload response (only metadata available)
- **Other useful fields**: `has_vocal`, `inferred_description` (contains tempo, key, instrumentation)

### 4. Auth Mechanism

- **No Authorization header** — Suno uses a custom `browser-token` header instead
- **Header location**: `browser-token: <redacted>` (sent on every protected request)
- **CSRF**: None visible (likely stored in browser session)
- **Cookies**: Not present in HAR (likely session cookies handled by browser)
- **Token refresh**: No explicit `/auth/refresh` endpoint visible in this session

### 5. Session State

- **Workspace/Project ID**: `75a360f7-44c2-4ed6-89f0-f25dcffe16b1` (appears in project/clips endpoints)
- **Upload ID**: Auto-generated by initial POST, used for polling
- **Clip ID**: Generated by `initialize-clip/` endpoint, different from upload ID
- **No pre-flight session bootstrap required** — Upload can start immediately with `browser-token`

### 6. Rate Limits / Retry Behavior

- **No 429/503 responses** in this session (all 2xx or 204)
- **No explicit rate limit headers** (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After absent)
- **Polling pattern**: Client polls GET every ~1-2 seconds until status = "complete"
- **Status progression**: `processing` → `passed_audio_processing` → `passed_artist_moderation` → `complete`

### 7. Third-Party Services

- **S3 upload**: Goes through AWS S3 (suno-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com) with SigV4
- **Analytics/logging**: Datadog RUM, TikTok pixel, Microsoft Clarity (safe to ignore for reproduction)
- **CDN**: Akamai and CloudFront for images/assets

### 8. API Classification

- **Internal frontend API**: Patterns like `studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/...` suggest internal API
- **Not publicly documented** (no OpenAPI/Swagger found in HAR)
- Uses domain `studio-api-prod` (production internal service), not a public v1/v2 API

### 9. Minimum Viable Curl

```bash
# Step 1: Create upload session
curl -X POST 'https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H "browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Origin: https://suno.com' \
  -H 'Referer: https://suno.com/' \
  -d '{"extension":"mp3"}' \
  -o upload_response.json

# Extract from response: id, url, fields (policy, signature, AWSAccessKeyId, key)

# Step 2: Upload to S3 (using pre-signed URL)
curl -X POST 'https://suno-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com/' \
  -F 'Content-Type=audio/mpeg' \
  -F "key=raw_uploads/${UPLOAD_ID}.mp3" \
  -F "AWSAccessKeyId=${AWS_ACCESS_KEY}" \
  -F "policy=${POLICY}" \
  -F "signature=${SIGNATURE}" \
  -F "file=@/path/to/audio.mp3"

# Step 3: Notify upload complete
curl -X POST \
  "https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/${UPLOAD_ID}/upload-finish/" \
  -H "browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d "{\"upload_type\":\"file_upload\",\"upload_filename\":\"audio.mp3\"}"

# Step 4: Poll for completion (repeat until status = "complete")
curl -X GET \
  "https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/${UPLOAD_ID}/" \
  -H "browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN"

# Step 5: Initialize clip (optional, to create a usable clip)
curl -X POST \
  "https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/${UPLOAD_ID}/initialize-clip/" \
  -H "browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{}'
```

### 10. Feasibility Verdict

**Ranking (easiest to hardest):**

**(A) Curl with exported session cookies — EASIEST**
- No auth header rotation or CSRF complexity
- Single `browser-token` header good for entire session
- Direct S3 SigV4 upload avoids streaming complexity
- All status codes are success (no error handling needed for happy path)

**(B) Playwright / headless browser automation — MEDIUM**
- Overkill for stateless API calls
- Adds overhead: browser startup, JS execution, cookie extraction
- Useful only if `browser-token` changes frequently or is derived from JavaScript

**(C) Chrome-devtools-mcp / live session — HARDEST**
- Requires user's browser to stay open
- Adds latency (browser <→ devtools protocol)
- No benefit over (A) unless token rotation is mandatory per request

**Verdict**: Approach (A) is cleanest. You only need the `browser-token` (extract from DevTools Network tab), then script the 5 HTTP calls. No CSRF complexity, no token expiration handling visible, and S3 pre-signed URLs handle authentication server-side.

---

**Key blocker check**: None detected. The API is straightforward, no Cloudflare Turnstile, no WebSocket upgrade, no per-request CSRF token regeneration.

2. **Never copy the file into the repo.** Read it in place from `C:/Users/odiem/Downloads/`.

4. **Redact any personally identifying info** — email addresses, real names, account IDs. If you can't tell if something is sensitive, err on the side of redacting.

## What to find

The user uploaded an audio file (`1774630111-jacob-collier-improvises-with.mp3`) to Suno's interface. Suno analyzed it and returned a Style block + Lyrics. I need to understand the network flow that made this happen so we can reproduce it in a CLI tool.

Answer these questions in your report:

### 1. Endpoint discovery
Which HTTP requests were made during the upload-analyze-response cycle? I'm looking for:
- The upload endpoint (POST, probably multipart/form-data with the mp3)
- Any intermediate endpoints (pre-signed URL request, upload token, job creation)
- The polling or webhook endpoint (if analysis is async)
- The final metadata fetch (where the Style and Lyrics come back)

For each, give: HTTP method, URL (with path, redact query params if they contain tokens), a 1-line purpose guess.

### 2. Request shape for the upload
- Content-Type header
- Body structure (multipart fields with names, JSON body with keys, etc.)
- Any hidden/important headers (X-Request-ID, X-CSRF, Accept, User-Agent)

### 3. Response shape for the final metadata call
This is the one we care about most — the response that contains the Style and Lyrics. Give:
- Top-level JSON keys
- Where exactly "Style" lives in the response (path like `data.analysis.style_prompt`)
- Where "Lyrics" lives
- Any other useful fields (tempo, key, duration, confidence, genre guesses)

If the response is not JSON, say so and describe what it is.

### 4. Auth mechanism
- Does Suno use Bearer tokens, session cookies, or both?
- Are tokens in the `Authorization` header, a custom header, or cookies?
- Do any of the requests have a CSRF token? If so, where does it come from (meta tag, cookie, separate endpoint)?
- What cookies are being sent on the protected requests (names only, not values)?
- Is there a token refresh flow visible in the HAR (e.g., `/auth/refresh` or similar)?

### 5. Session state
- Is there a workspace ID, user ID, or account ID embedded in URLs or headers?
- Is there a "session" or "workspace" concept that needs to be bootstrapped before upload?

### 6. Rate limits / retry behavior
- Any 429s, 503s, or retry-with-backoff patterns in the HAR?
- Any response headers suggesting rate limits (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After)?

### 7. Third-party services
- Does the upload go directly to Suno, or is it proxied through AWS S3 / Cloudflare R2 / etc.?
- Are there any external service calls (analytics, error tracking) that we can ignore?

### 8. Is this a documented/public API?
- Does the URL pattern suggest it's a public API (`api.suno.com/v1/...`) or an internal frontend API (`suno.com/api/...`)?
- Any `OpenAPI`, `swagger`, or documentation URLs referenced?

### 9. Minimum viable curl
Based on what you find, sketch the rough shape of a curl command that would reproduce the upload. DO NOT include real auth values — use placeholders like `$SUNO_SESSION_COOKIE`. Something like:

```
curl -X POST 'https://suno.com/api/upload' \
  -H 'Cookie: sess=$SUNO_SESSION_COOKIE' \
  -F 'audio=@/path/to/file.mp3' \
  -F 'analyze=true'
```

If it's more complex (pre-signed S3 URL flow, websocket upgrade, multi-step), describe the steps.

### 10. Feasibility verdict
Based on everything you found, rank the three approach options from easiest to hardest for reproducing this programmatically:

- **(A)** Curl with exported session cookies
- **(B)** Playwright / headless browser automation
- **(C)** chrome-devtools-mcp piggybacking on user's live session

Give a 1-2 sentence reason for the ranking. If there's a serious blocker for any approach (CSRF token regenerated per-request from a meta tag, Cloudflare turnstile, websocket upgrade required), flag it.

## Output format

A structured markdown report with sections 1-10 above. Be thorough but do NOT paste large raw bodies. If a request/response is interesting, summarize its shape; don't include the full JSON.

Under 1500 words. The report should be something I can read and decide "yes, go build this" or "this is harder than I thought, we need a different approach."

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me create a comprehensive report:

## Suno Upload-Audio API Analysis Report

### 1. Endpoint Discovery

The upload-analyze-response cycle consists of these HTTP requests:

| Method | URL | Purpose |
|--------|-----|---------|
| POST | `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/` | Create upload session, get S3 pre-signed URL |
| POST | `https://suno-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com/` | Upload MP3 directly to AWS S3 |
| POST | `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/{id}/upload-finish/` | Notify server upload complete |
| GET | `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/{id}/` | Poll analysis progress (called 5x) |
| POST | `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/{id}/initialize-clip/` | Create clip from upload (returns `clip_id`) |

All requests return 200 or 204 (success).

### 2. Request Shape for Upload

**Initial POST to create upload session:**
- **Content-Type**: `application/json`
- **Body**: `{"extension":"mp3"}`
- **Key headers**: `browser-token: <redacted>`, `origin: https://suno.com`, `referer: https://suno.com/`

**Response**: Returns `id`, `url` (S3 bucket), `fields` (form data for S3), `is_file_uploaded`

**S3 Upload (multipart/form-data):**
- Uses AWS SigV4 pre-signed POST
- Form fields: `Content-Type`, `key`, `AWSAccessKeyId`, `policy`, `signature`, `file` (binary MP3)
- Response: 204 No Content

**Upload-finish POST:**
- **Body**: `{"upload_type":"file_upload","upload_filename":"1774630111-jacob-collier-improvises-with.mp3"}`

### 3. Response Shape for Final Metadata

**Polling GET response (when status = "complete")** returns:
```json
{
  "id": "e9c6d2e0-c3b6-4a4e-92e2-d025282e94d6",
  "status": "complete",
  "s3_id": "bd90d2da-438c-43f7-8e04-8f952209474e",
  "title": "1774630111-jacob-collier-improvises-with",
  "image_url": "https://cdn2.suno.ai/image_{s3_id}.jpeg",
  "has_vocal": true,
  "display_tags": "alternative rock, britpop, male falsetto vocals",
  "inferred_description": "[Long AI-generated description of audio characteristics, tempo, key, production details]"
}
```

**Key locations:**
- **Style**: `display_tags` (comma-separated)
- **Lyrics**: Not returned in upload response (only metadata available)
- **Other useful fields**: `has_vocal`, `inferred_description` (contains tempo, key, instrumentation)

### 4. Auth Mechanism

- **No Authorization header** — Suno uses a custom `browser-token` header instead
- **Header location**: `browser-token: <redacted>` (sent on every protected request)
- **CSRF**: None visible (likely stored in browser session)
- **Cookies**: Not present in HAR (likely session cookies handled by browser)
- **Token refresh**: No explicit `/auth/refresh` endpoint visible in this session

### 5. Session State

- **Workspace/Project ID**: `75a360f7-44c2-4ed6-89f0-f25dcffe16b1` (appears in project/clips endpoints)
- **Upload ID**: Auto-generated by initial POST, used for polling
- **Clip ID**: Generated by `initialize-clip/` endpoint, different from upload ID
- **No pre-flight session bootstrap required** — Upload can start immediately with `browser-token`

### 6. Rate Limits / Retry Behavior

- **No 429/503 responses** in this session (all 2xx or 204)
- **No explicit rate limit headers** (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After absent)
- **Polling pattern**: Client polls GET every ~1-2 seconds until status = "complete"
- **Status progression**: `processing` → `passed_audio_processing` → `passed_artist_moderation` → `complete`

### 7. Third-Party Services

- **S3 upload**: Goes through AWS S3 (suno-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com) with SigV4
- **Analytics/logging**: Datadog RUM, TikTok pixel, Microsoft Clarity (safe to ignore for reproduction)
- **CDN**: Akamai and CloudFront for images/assets

### 8. API Classification

- **Internal frontend API**: Patterns like `studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/...` suggest internal API
- **Not publicly documented** (no OpenAPI/Swagger found in HAR)
- Uses domain `studio-api-prod` (production internal service), not a public v1/v2 API

### 9. Minimum Viable Curl

```bash
# Step 1: Create upload session
curl -X POST 'https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H "browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Origin: https://suno.com' \
  -H 'Referer: https://suno.com/' \
  -d '{"extension":"mp3"}' \
  -o upload_response.json

# Extract from response: id, url, fields (policy, signature, AWSAccessKeyId, key)

# Step 2: Upload to S3 (using pre-signed URL)
curl -X POST 'https://suno-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com/' \
  -F 'Content-Type=audio/mpeg' \
  -F "key=raw_uploads/${UPLOAD_ID}.mp3" \
  -F "AWSAccessKeyId=${AWS_ACCESS_KEY}" \
  -F "policy=${POLICY}" \
  -F "signature=${SIGNATURE}" \
  -F "file=@/path/to/audio.mp3"

# Step 3: Notify upload complete
curl -X POST \
  "https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/${UPLOAD_ID}/upload-finish/" \
  -H "browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d "{\"upload_type\":\"file_upload\",\"upload_filename\":\"audio.mp3\"}"

# Step 4: Poll for completion (repeat until status = "complete")
curl -X GET \
  "https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/${UPLOAD_ID}/" \
  -H "browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN"

# Step 5: Initialize clip (optional, to create a usable clip)
curl -X POST \
  "https://studio-api-prod.suno.com/api/uploads/audio/${UPLOAD_ID}/initialize-clip/" \
  -H "browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{}'
```

### 10. Feasibility Verdict

**Ranking (easiest to hardest):**

**(A) Curl with exported session cookies — EASIEST**
- No auth header rotation or CSRF complexity
- Single `browser-token` header good for entire session
- Direct S3 SigV4 upload avoids streaming complexity
- All status codes are success (no error handling needed for happy path)

**(B) Playwright / headless browser automation — MEDIUM**
- Overkill for stateless API calls
- Adds overhead: browser startup, JS execution, cookie extraction
- Useful only if `browser-token` changes frequently or is derived from JavaScript

**(C) Chrome-devtools-mcp / live session — HARDEST**
- Requires user's browser to stay open
- Adds latency (browser <→ devtools protocol)
- No benefit over (A) unless token rotation is mandatory per request

**Verdict**: Approach (A) is cleanest. You only need the `browser-token` (extract from DevTools Network tab), then script the 5 HTTP calls. No CSRF complexity, no token expiration handling visible, and S3 pre-signed URLs handle authentication server-side.

---

**Key blocker check**: None detected. The API is straightforward, no Cloudflare Turnstile, no WebSocket upgrade, no per-request CSRF token regeneration.

content.text:
## HAR analysis — the path is clear, but there's a wrinkle

### The good news

Suno's upload flow is a clean 5-step API sequence with no CSRF, no token refresh, no websocket upgrade, no Cloudflare turnstile. The auth is a single `browser-token` header that your session has. This is **very doable** with plain curl + bun:

1. `POST /api/uploads/audio/` with `{"extension":"mp3"}` → returns S3 pre-signed URL + upload ID
2. `POST` direct to S3 with the audio file (multipart) → 204
3. `POST /api/uploads/audio/{id}/upload-finish/` to notify completion
4. `GET /api/uploads/audio/{id}/` (poll every 1–2s) until status becomes `complete`
5. Optional: `POST /api/uploads/audio/{id}/initialize-clip/` to create a usable clip

**Approach A (curl + bun with exported browser-token) wins cleanly.** No need for Playwright or chrome-devtools-mcp. Simpler, single-file tool, no browser dependency.

### The wrinkle — and it's important

**The upload response contains metadata, but not lyrics.** Here's what actually comes back:

| Field | Contents | Maps to |
|---|---|---|
| `display_tags` | `"alternative rock, britpop, male falsetto vocals"` | Comma-separated style tags |
| `inferred_description` | Long prose description with tempo, key, instrumentation, dynamics | **This is the rich prose Style block you saw** |
| `has_vocal` | `true` | Boolean vocal detection |
| `title` | Filename (not a track title) | Not useful |

**`lyrics` is NOT in this response.** Which means the lyrics you saw populated in Suno's UI for the Jacob Collier track...

### The lyrics question — my hypothesis

**I don't think Suno is transcribing audio to lyrics.** I think Suno is **matching the audio against a known-track database** (Coldplay's "Yellow" is one of the most recognizable songs of the 2000s), and when it hits, it pulls the lyrics from their library.

Evidence for this hypothesis:
- The HAR captures the full upload/analyze cycle but has **zero lyrics payload** anywhere in the responses
- The lyrics you showed me are **verbatim Coldplay Yellow** — not a transcription of Jacob Collier's improvisation on top of it
- Suno already has a known track-matching capability (it's built into their copyright detection)
- Audio-to-lyric transcription for mixed instrumentation is hard and Suno doesn't claim that feature publicly

**If this hypothesis is right, the tool's value prop changes:**

✅ **Reliably extract Style prose (`inferred_description`) from any audio** — universal, works for your own custom files, works for BGM, works for instrumental tracks
✅ **Reliably extract style tags (`display_tags`)** — same
✅ **Detect vocals (`has_vocal`)** — same
⚠️ **Extract lyrics only works for already-released, famous tracks** — won't work on your own generations, won't work on obscure reference tracks

This doesn't kill the tool — the Style extraction alone is the high-value part. Music cards benefit hugely from having Suno's own prose description attached. But it does mean we shouldn't promise "lyrics + style" — we should promise "Style + metadata, with Lyrics as a bonus when Suno recognizes the track."

### Two ways to verify before I build anything

**Cheap verification:** upload a track Suno definitely doesn't know (a Tala generation, an obscure YouTube upload, your own humming) and check if Suno's UI populates the Lyrics field. If it stays empty or says "couldn't extract lyrics", hypothesis confirmed.

**Medium verification:** run the same flow in your browser DevTools, capture another HAR of an unknown-track upload, and compare. If you have the time, this is the definitive answer.

**Before I spend Ryan's time building the tool, I'd really like to know which it is.** The tool scope is meaningfully different: "Style + Lyrics extractor" vs. "Style extractor with opportunistic lyrics for known tracks."

---

## Proposed tool scope (once the lyrics question is resolved)

### Tool name: `suno-extract` (or `generate-suno-meta` if you prefer)

### Responsibilities

| Phase | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Auth** | Reads `SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN` from env var or `.env.local`. Fails loud with a clear message if missing. Does NOT fetch the token itself — that's the user's responsibility via DevTools copy-paste. |
| **Upload** | Runs the 5-step flow. Uploads the audio file, polls until complete, returns the raw metadata blob. |
| **Parse** | Extracts `display_tags`, `inferred_description`, `has_vocal`, and any lyrics if present. Handles the "no lyrics" case gracefully. |
| **Output** | Two modes: (a) JSON dump to stdout for pipeline use, (b) pretty format for human reading. |

### Integration with music cards (separate concern, phase 2)

Once the extractor tool exists and works, **enhance `/create-music-card`** to optionally call it as a new pipeline step:

```
yt-dlp → ffmpeg trim → librosa analysis → Gemini refinement → [NEW] suno-extract → write music card
```

The music card then has both the measured data AND Suno's own semantic reading. New frontmatter fields:
- `suno_style_tags`: string (from `display_tags`)
- `suno_description`: string (from `inferred_description`, the prose block)
- `suno_has_vocal`: boolean
- `suno_extraction_date`: ISO date

This is **additive** — existing music cards keep working, new ones get richer.

### Explicitly out of scope (v1)

- Automated token refresh (browser-token extraction from a running Chrome session)
- Batch extraction across a folder
- Integration with `/create-track` (that's a different question — does Tala learn from Suno extractions?)
- Fine-grained retry/error classification
- Non-MP3 audio formats (v1 is MP3-only, extensions can come later)

### Token extraction UX for v1

User flow to get the `browser-token`:
1. Open suno.com in Chrome while logged in
2. Open DevTools → Network tab
3. Trigger any request (e.g., navigate to create page)
4. Find any request to `studio-api-prod.suno.com`
5. Copy the `browser-token` header value
6. `export SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN="<value>"` or save to `.env.local`

Not elegant, but v1-acceptable. If the token turns out to expire frequently, we upgrade to a helper command later.

---

## My recommended path forward

**Right now:** answer the lyrics question. Either:
- `[A]` Upload a non-famous track (your own Tala generation, your own voice memo, anything) in your Suno browser session RIGHT NOW and tell me what the Lyrics field does
- `[B]` I trust the hypothesis and we build "Style extractor" scope (lyrics-opportunistic)
- `[C]` Capture a second HAR from a non-famous track upload and I'll re-analyze

**Then (if we're building it):**
1. I (Freddie) finalize the scope based on what we learn
2. Dispatch **McCall** to write a task doc (file-by-file spec, function signatures, error handling, test cases)
3. Dispatch **Ryan** to implement
4. Dispatch **McCall** to review Ryan's output
5. Commit + push

This is a legit infrastructure task and it follows the `.claude/rules/development.md` pattern. It's not a one-line hack, it's a new CLI tool with API integration and music-card pipeline implications.

**Which option — A, B, or C?** And a quick tangential question: do you want the tool name to be `suno-extract` or `generate-suno-meta`? (I slightly prefer `suno-extract` for the punchiness, but it's your toolkit.)

content.text:
My lyrics-database hypothesis was wrong, and I was wrong to be overconfident about it. Thank you for the correction — this is exactly why we verify before building.

## What actually happens (the missing two steps)

The upload flow has **7 steps**, not 5. The explorer missed the last two because they happen on a different URL base (`/api/gen/` instead of `/api/uploads/`):

| # | Method | Endpoint | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | POST | `/api/uploads/audio/` | Create upload session, get S3 URL |
| 2 | POST | `suno-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com` | Upload audio to S3 |
| 3 | POST | `/api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/upload-finish/` | Notify upload complete |
| 4 | GET | `/api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/` (poll) | Wait for `status: complete` |
| 5 | POST | `/api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/initialize-clip/` | Create clip, get `clip_id` |
| 6 | POST | `/api/gen/{clip_id}/set_metadata/` | **Returns lyrics in `prompt` field** |
| 7 | POST | `/api/gen/{clip_id}/set_audio_description/` | **Returns style prose in `metadata.tags` field** |

**Crucial correction:** `initialize-clip` is **mandatory**, not optional as the explorer suggested. Steps 6 and 7 are keyed by `clip_id` (not `upload_id`), so without step 5 we can't reach the good stuff.

## What we can actually extract (per sample)

From step 6 response — `prompt` field:
- **Full lyrics with typed brackets** — intro/verse/chorus markers, inline instrument direction, mid-line vocal markers (`[falsetto]`)
- This IS Suno's transcription of the audio, including action-based brackets

From step 7 response — `metadata.tags` field:
- **Full prose Style block** — the rich sentence-based description with arrangement, dynamics, tempo, key
- Plus `metadata.duration`, `metadata.has_vocal`, and the shorter `display_tags` at the top level

This is **everything we need** for a music card. Both lyrics AND style, directly from Suno's own interpretation.

## One missing piece before Ryan can build

The response shapes for steps 6 and 7 are clear (you pasted them). What I still need to know is the **request shape** — do those POSTs take an empty body, a specific JSON payload, or something else? Let me verify.

**HAR file:** `C:/Users/odiem/Downloads/suno.com.har`

**Same credential rules as before:** never echo auth values, bearer tokens, browser-tokens, cookies, user IDs, or session IDs. Redact real IDs in URLs (replace with placeholders like `{clip_id}`). Do not paste any personally identifying info.

content.text:
## Everything's locked in — here's the final picture

### The 7-step flow with all request bodies

| # | Method | Endpoint | Request body | Response contains |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | POST | `/api/uploads/audio/` | `{"extension":"mp3"}` | `id`, S3 `url`, S3 signing `fields` |
| 2 | POST | `suno-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com` | multipart (S3 fields + `file=@audio.mp3`) | 204 |
| 3 | POST | `/api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/upload-finish/` | `{"upload_type":"file_upload","upload_filename":"<name>.mp3"}` | ack |
| 4 | GET | `/api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/` (poll) | none | `status` progression until `complete` |
| 5 | POST | `/api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/initialize-clip/` | `{}` | full clip object with **new `clip_id`** and `image_url` |
| 6 | POST | `/api/gen/{clip_id}/set_metadata/` (**trailing slash**) | `{"title":"<name>","image_url":"<from step 5>","is_audio_upload_tos_accepted":true}` | `prompt` = **full lyrics with typed brackets** |
| 7 | POST | `/api/gen/{clip_id}/set_audio_description` (**NO trailing slash**) | `{"gemini_description_accepted":true}` | `metadata.tags` = **full prose Style** |

### The non-obvious discoveries

**1. Steps 6 and 7 are acknowledgments, not generators.** Suno generates the lyrics and the prose Style block **during polling (step 4)**, using Gemini on their backend. Steps 6 and 7 are the UI saying "yes, save this" and Suno returning the already-computed values as a side-effect of the save. This is good news — it means both calls are fast, synchronous, and return everything we need in a single round-trip each.

**2. The trailing-slash asymmetry is real.** `set_metadata/` has a trailing slash. `set_audio_description` does NOT. Django REST Framework does this sometimes. **This will bite us if we template the URLs.** The task doc must call this out explicitly.

**3. `device-id` is a second required header** alongside `browser-token`. The first explorer pass missed it because it only flagged `browser-token` as the auth. Both are extracted from the browser session, both go in every protected request.

**4. Both endpoints are retry-safe** (the HAR shows 3 copies of each, which means the user's browser retried and got consistent 200s). No special idempotency handling needed.

**5. My lyrics-database hypothesis was flat-out wrong.** Suno IS generating lyrics from the audio (probably transcription + track-matching for famous songs, pure transcription otherwise). This means **the tool will extract lyrics from your own Tala generations too** — not just famous reference tracks. That's significantly better than I feared.

---

## Final tool scope (ready for McCall)

### Name: `suno-extract`

Chose the punchier one unless you veto. Easy to rename later if you want `generate-suno-meta`.

### Responsibilities (v1)

| Concern | v1 behavior |
|---|---|
| **Input** | Single local audio file path (mp3 only for v1) |
| **Auth** | Read `SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN` and `SUNO_DEVICE_ID` from env (`.env.local`). Fail with actionable error if either is missing or expired. |
| **Upload flow** | All 7 steps, in order. Poll step 4 with 1s interval, 90s timeout. |
| **Output** | Two modes: `--json` (full structured payload for pipeline use) or default pretty format (markdown-ready). |
| **Fields returned** | `lyrics` (from step 6 `prompt`), `style_prose` (from step 7 `metadata.tags`), `display_tags` (short style), `duration`, `has_vocal`, `clip_id`, `audio_url` (Suno's hosted version) |
| **Error handling** | Fail loudly. Specific error messages for: missing token, 401 (expired), 4xx (user error), 5xx (retry once then give up), polling timeout, unexpected response shape |
| **Retries** | S3 upload: 3 retries with exponential backoff. Polling: handled by the poll itself. Other calls: no retry (fail fast so user knows something's wrong). |

### Explicitly out of scope (v1)

- Automated token/device-id extraction from a running Chrome session
- Batch mode (processing a folder of files)
- Non-mp3 formats
- Integration with `/create-music-card` (separate task after v1 lands)
- Generating thumbnails or audio URLs for embedding
- Token expiration detection / refresh

### Music card integration — v2, separate task

Once v1 is proven, enhance `/create-music-card` to call `suno-extract` as a new pipeline step. Adds three frontmatter fields:
- `suno_style_prose`: the rich prose Style block
- `suno_lyrics`: the bracket-annotated lyrics
- `suno_display_tags`: the short comma-separated tags

This is **additive** — existing music cards keep working, new ones get richer. Should be a small McCall→Ryan cycle once the extractor is stable.

### Token extraction UX (v1)

User workflow to bootstrap:

```
1. Open suno.com in Chrome while logged in
2. F12 → Network tab → upload any audio in the UI
3. Click any request to studio-api-prod.suno.com
4. Copy the browser-token header value
5. Copy the device-id header value
6. Write both to .env.local:
   SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN=<value>
   SUNO_DEVICE_ID=<value>
```

Not elegant, but v1-acceptable. If the token expires frequently enough to be annoying, we upgrade to a helper command that reads from a running Chrome session via CDP — but that's v2 based on evidence, not speculation.

---

## Three questions before I dispatch McCall

**Q1 — Tool name:** `suno-extract` (punchy) or `generate-suno-meta` (descriptive)? My slight lean is `suno-extract`.

**Q2 — Music card integration sequencing:** Do you want:
- **(a)** Ship v1 extractor standalone, prove it works, THEN enhance `/create-music-card` as a follow-up task (my recommendation — smaller PRs, easier rollback)
- **(b)** Bundle both in one task (extractor + music-card integration) so the workflow is complete in one cycle

**Q3 — Scope verification:** Anything I missed or got wrong in the scope above? Anything you want added to v1 or explicitly removed?

Once you answer these, I'll dispatch **McCall** to write the task doc (file-by-file spec, function signatures, error cases, test plan), then **Ryan** to implement, then **McCall** to review. That's the full dev flow per `.claude/rules/development.md`.

message.content:
Q1. suno-extract-meta
Q2. a
Q3. is extracting cookie jar? or that just add complication?

content.text:
## Q3 answer — skip the cookie jar, your instinct is right

**Important factual correction first:** Suno's auth isn't actually in cookies. The two required values — `browser-token` and `device-id` — are **custom HTTP headers**, not cookies. Chrome sends them from localStorage/IndexedDB, not the cookie jar. So "extracting the cookie jar" wouldn't help us here even if we tried.

To automate extraction we'd have three options, and **all three add disproportionate complication** for a tool you'll run a few times per week:

| Option | What it needs | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Read Chrome localStorage directly** | Parse Chrome profile paths (platform-specific), handle SQLite leveldb, deal with Chrome encrypting values in newer versions, pick the right profile if multiple | 1–2 days of work, breaks on Chrome updates |
| **Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP)** | Chrome launched with `--remote-debugging-port` flag every time, or attach to an existing one. User has to remember to use a special launcher. | Changes your Chrome launch workflow. Fragile. |
| **chrome-devtools-mcp** | Extra MCP dependency, tied to MCP infrastructure, needs live Chrome session | Adds a heavy integration for a 30-second save |

**My recommendation: skip it all.** Manual copy-paste is ~30 seconds one time, and the token probably lasts hours or days. When it expires, another 30 seconds. That's a rounding error against the value of the tool.

### What I'll add to v1 instead — two small quality-of-life helpers

Both are cheap (half a day of Ryan's time combined) and genuinely useful:

1. **`suno-extract-meta --check-token`** — validates the current token against Suno's API without running a full upload. Returns "token valid, expires approximately X" or "token expired/invalid, here's how to refresh." Saves you from burning an upload to discover auth is stale.

2. **`suno-extract-meta --setup-help`** — prints the exact copy-paste instructions (which headers, where to find them, what file to write them to) inline in the terminal. New or occasional user doesn't have to hunt for the docs.

Neither requires browser automation. Both are pure CLI.

**If manual token refresh becomes annoying in practice,** we upgrade to automated extraction as v2 — but only with evidence that the manual flow is a real friction point, not speculation. Same discipline we applied to ASL: ship the simple thing, measure, then decide.

---

## Scope locked

| Item | Decision |
|---|---|
| **Name** | `suno-extract-meta` |
| **Sequencing** | v1 standalone first, music-card integration as follow-up task |
| **Auth extraction** | Manual copy-paste, with `--check-token` and `--setup-help` helpers. No browser automation. |
| **Input** | Single mp3 file path, v1 |
| **Output modes** | `--json` (full payload) and default (pretty markdown) |
| **Fields returned** | lyrics, style_prose, display_tags, duration, has_vocal, clip_id, audio_url |
| **Error handling** | Fail loud, specific messages per failure mode |
| **Retries** | S3 upload: 3x exponential backoff. Other calls: fail fast. |
| **Out of scope** | Batch mode, non-mp3, automated auth, music-card integration, token refresh automation |

---

Dispatching **McCall** now to write the task doc. He'll produce the file-by-file spec, function signatures, TypeScript types for the request/response payloads, error case enumeration, and test plan. Expect a ~2 day task estimate based on the scope.

input.prompt:
You are writing a task doc for Ryan to implement a new CLI tool. Read `.claude/souls/mccall.md` first for your architecture reflexes.

## Task

Build `suno-extract-meta` — a CLI tool that uploads a local mp3 to Suno's upload-audio endpoint, runs the full 7-step extraction flow, and returns Suno's own Lyrics + Style interpretation of the audio. This unlocks Suno's internal prose Style format and lyric transcription for use in Tala's music card pipeline.

**Name:** `suno-extract-meta`
**Location:** `src/tools/suno-extract-meta.ts` (single file tool per existing tool runner pattern)
**Estimated effort:** 2 days

## Background

Session 2026-04-09: user discovered Suno has a new "upload audio" feature that analyzes an uploaded track and auto-populates Lyrics + Style. The extracted values round-trip well — the user tested by regenerating from the extracted values and got a close match to the original audio. This is the first time we've had access to Suno's own semantic interpretation of a track. Previously we only had measured data (BPM, key, spectral features via librosa) plus hand-written Tala craft descriptors.

The flow was reverse-engineered from a real HAR file (`C:/Users/odiem/Downloads/suno.com.har`, not committed to repo, contains real session auth). Two explorer passes mapped the complete 7-step flow with all request/response shapes.

Tool is v1 standalone. Music card integration is a follow-up task (do NOT include in this task).

## The 7-step flow (spec)

All endpoints use `https://studio-api-prod.suno.com` unless noted.

### Required headers on all Suno API calls

- `browser-token: $SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN` (from env)
- `device-id: $SUNO_DEVICE_ID` (from env)
- `origin: https://suno.com`
- `referer: https://suno.com/`
- `content-type: application/json` (on JSON POSTs)
- `accept: application/json`

### Step 1: Create upload session
```
POST /api/uploads/audio/
Body: {"extension":"mp3"}
Returns: {
  "id": "<upload_id>",
  "url": "<S3 endpoint URL>",
  "fields": { ...S3 signing fields... },
  "is_file_uploaded": false
}
```

### Step 2: Upload to S3
```
POST <url from step 1>   (NOT Suno's API — this goes to AWS S3)
Content-Type: multipart/form-data
Form fields: (all fields from step 1's `fields` object) + "file": <audio binary>
Returns: 204 No Content
```

**Critical:** no `browser-token` on this call. S3 uses its own SigV4 pre-signed POST. Fields from step 1 include `Content-Type`, `key`, `AWSAccessKeyId`, `policy`, `signature` — all must be passed through exactly.

### Step 3: Notify upload complete
```
POST /api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/upload-finish/
Body: {"upload_type":"file_upload","upload_filename":"<original filename>.mp3"}
Returns: ack
```

### Step 4: Poll for analysis completion
```
GET /api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/
Returns: { "id": "...", "status": "<progression>", ... }
```

Poll every 1000ms, timeout after 90s. Status progression: `processing` → `passed_audio_processing` → `passed_artist_moderation` → `complete`. Only proceed when `status === "complete"`.

### Step 5: Initialize clip
```
POST /api/uploads/audio/{upload_id}/initialize-clip/
Body: {}
Returns: {
  "id": "<CLIP_ID — this is a NEW ID, different from upload_id>",
  "title": "<filename>",
  "image_url": "https://cdn2.suno.ai/image_<clip_id>.jpeg",
  ...
}
```

**The clip_id from this step is what steps 6 and 7 use. Do not reuse upload_id.**

### Step 6: Fetch lyrics (trailing slash MANDATORY)
```
POST /api/gen/{clip_id}/set_metadata/    ← NOTE the trailing slash
Body: {
  "title": "<filename>",
  "image_url": "<image_url from step 5>",
  "is_audio_upload_tos_accepted": true
}
Returns: {
  "id": "<clip_id>",
  "title": "...",
  "image_url": "...",
  "prompt": "<FULL LYRICS WITH TYPED BRACKETS — this is what we want>",
  "video_is_stale": false,
  "opt_out_video_cover_hook": false
}
```

The `prompt` field is Suno's extracted lyrics. This IS an acknowledgment call — Suno already generated the lyrics during step 4 polling using Gemini on their backend. This call "accepts and saves" them and returns them as side-effect.

### Step 7: Fetch style prose (NO trailing slash)
```
POST /api/gen/{clip_id}/set_audio_description    ← NOTE: NO trailing slash
Body: {"gemini_description_accepted": true}
Returns: {
  "status": "complete",
  "id": "<clip_id>",
  "title": "...",
  "audio_url": "https://cdn1.suno.ai/<clip_id>.mp3",
  "image_url": "...",
  "metadata": {
    "tags": "<FULL PROSE STYLE BLOCK — this is what we want>",
    "prompt": "<lyrics, same as step 6>",
    "type": "upload",
    "duration": 96.0,
    "has_vocal": true,
    ...
  },
  "display_tags": "alternative rock, britpop, male falsetto vocals",
  ...
}
```

The `metadata.tags` field is Suno's rich prose Style block. `display_tags` at top level is the short comma-separated tag list.

**Critical gotcha:** the trailing slash differs between steps 6 and 7. Step 6 has it, step 7 does not. This is a Django REST Framework artifact and it WILL break if templated naively.

## Tool responsibilities

| Concern | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Input | Single positional arg: local mp3 file path. Validate exists and is mp3 before any network calls. |
| Auth | Read `SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN` and `SUNO_DEVICE_ID` from env. Support `.env.local` via existing dotenv pattern if the codebase uses one; else plain env. Fail with actionable error if either is missing. |
| Upload flow | All 7 steps in order. Poll step 4 with 1000ms interval, 90s timeout. |
| Output modes | Default: pretty markdown format showing lyrics, style prose, display tags, duration, has_vocal, audio_url. `--json`: single JSON object with all extracted fields for pipeline use. |
| Helper commands | `--check-token`: validate token/device-id against Suno (e.g., make a cheap authenticated GET that 401s if auth is bad) without running a full upload. `--setup-help`: print step-by-step copy-paste instructions for extracting the two headers from Chrome DevTools. |
| Error handling | Fail loud, specific messages per failure mode. See error catalog below. |
| Retries | S3 upload: 3 retries with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s). Other calls: fail fast (no retry). Polling is its own loop. |

## Error catalog (Ryan should implement specific messages for each)

| Condition | Message | Exit code |
|---|---|---|
| Missing env var | `"SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN is not set. Run 'suno-extract-meta --setup-help' for extraction instructions."` | 1 |
| File does not exist | `"Audio file not found: {path}"` | 1 |
| File is not mp3 | `"Only mp3 files are supported in v1. Got: {extension}"` | 1 |
| Step 1: 401 | `"Suno rejected auth. Token may be expired. Run 'suno-extract-meta --check-token' to verify, or --setup-help to refresh."` | 2 |
| Step 1: 4xx (non-401) | `"Suno API error on upload session creation: {status} {body snippet}"` | 3 |
| Step 1: 5xx | `"Suno API 5xx on upload session creation: {status}. Retry in a few moments."` | 4 |
| Step 2: S3 upload failure after 3 retries | `"S3 upload failed after 3 retries: {last error}"` | 5 |
| Step 4: polling timeout (90s) | `"Timed out waiting for Suno analysis to complete. Upload ID: {upload_id}. Last status: {status}."` | 6 |
| Step 6 or 7: unexpected response shape | `"Suno returned unexpected response shape at step {N}. Expected 'metadata.tags' / 'prompt'. Raw response logged to stderr."` | 7 |
| Unknown/unexpected error | `"Unexpected error: {message}. If this reproduces, capture HAR and open an issue."` | 99 |

## Output format specs

### Default pretty format

```
Suno extraction — {filename}
──────────────────────────────────────

Duration:    {seconds}s
Has vocal:   {yes/no}

─── Lyrics ────────────────────────────

{prompt verbatim — preserve newlines}

─── Raw IDs ───────────────────────────
Upload ID: {upload_id}
Clip ID:   {clip_id}
```

### JSON format (`--json`)

```typescript
interface SunoExtractResult {
  file: { path: string; filename: string; size_bytes: number };
  upload_id: string;
  clip_id: string;
  audio_url: string;   // Suno-hosted copy of our upload
  image_url: string;
  lyrics: string;       // from step 6 metadata.prompt
  style_prose: string;  // from step 7 metadata.tags
  display_tags: string; // short comma-separated
  duration_seconds: number;
  has_vocal: boolean;
  extracted_at: string; // ISO timestamp
}
```

## File scope

### Create

- `src/tools/suno-extract-meta.ts` — the CLI tool, single file, exports `toolDef` for runner autodiscovery
- `src/libs/suno-api/` — optional helper module IF the CLI file gets over ~400 lines. Contains typed interfaces for request/response shapes, the 7-step functions, and shared headers builder. Ryan's call based on code size at end of phase 1.
- `src/tools/__tests__/suno-extract-meta.test.ts` — unit tests with mocked fetch/undici calls, covering the happy path and every error case in the error catalog

### Read-only reference

## Architectural constraints

1. **Bun + TypeScript.** No Python, no Node scripts. Per `.claude/rules/command-execution.md`.
2. **Use `fetch`** (Bun's global) for HTTP calls. Do NOT add `axios`, `undici`, or `node-fetch`. Bun's fetch handles multipart and SigV4-pre-signed POSTs fine.
3. **No new dependencies** for v1. Use Bun stdlib only.
4. **Tool must be discoverable** by `bun run tool --list` via the `toolDef` export pattern.
5. **Fail closed.** Any unexpected response shape → exit with error, never silently skip or produce partial output.
6. **Handle the trailing-slash asymmetry** explicitly. The URL for set_metadata and set_audio_description should be two distinct constants, not one templated path. Comment the asymmetry.
7. **Do not log full response bodies to stdout** — they contain user_id, display_name, avatar_image_url, and other PII. Log them only to stderr at debug level (if a --debug flag is added; otherwise don't log them at all).
8. **Never commit the HAR file** used for this reverse-engineering work. It's in `C:/Users/odiem/Downloads/` and stays there. Do not copy it into the repo for reference.
9. **Token handling:** never print `SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN` or `SUNO_DEVICE_ID` to stdout or stderr. Ever. Even in debug output.

## Out of scope (explicitly)

- Music card pipeline integration (follow-up task after v1 is proven)
- Non-mp3 formats (wav, m4a, flac)
- Batch mode (processing a folder of files)
- Automated token extraction from a running Chrome session
- Token refresh flow
- Retry logic beyond S3 upload
- Thumbnail generation, waveform extraction, any other `/api/gen/{clip_id}/*` endpoints
- Any write operation to Suno (publishing, trashing, commenting)
- Progress bars, spinners, TTY detection
- Caching — every run makes fresh API calls, no local cache

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `bun run tool suno-extract-meta <path-to-mp3>` produces the pretty format output with all required fields populated, assuming valid env + valid mp3
- [ ] `bun run tool suno-extract-meta <path> --json` produces valid JSON matching the `SunoExtractResult` interface
- [ ] `bun run tool suno-extract-meta --check-token` returns success with a valid token, fails loudly with invalid
- [ ] `bun run tool suno-extract-meta --setup-help` prints copy-paste instructions without making any network calls
- [ ] `bun run tool suno-extract-meta --help` prints usage
- [ ] Missing `SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN` produces the env var error message and exit code 1
- [ ] Missing `SUNO_DEVICE_ID` produces the same treatment
- [ ] Non-existent file path produces the file-not-found error and exit 1
- [ ] Non-mp3 file produces the extension error and exit 1
- [ ] Expired token on step 1 produces the 401 message and exit 2
- [ ] S3 upload failure after 3 retries produces exit 5 with the last error
- [ ] 90s polling timeout produces exit 6 with upload_id and last status
- [ ] Unit tests cover happy path + every error case, using mocked fetch
- [ ] `bun run tool --list` shows the new tool
- [ ] `SUNO_BROWSER_TOKEN` and `SUNO_DEVICE_ID` NEVER appear in any log output, even in error messages
- [ ] No new dependencies added to `package.json`

3. Missing SUNO_DEVICE_ID → exit 1 with correct message

5. Non-mp3 extension → exit 1
6. Step 1 returns 401 → exit 2 with expired-token message
7. Step 1 returns 500 → exit 4
8. Step 2 S3 fails 3 times → exit 5 with last error
9. Step 2 S3 fails twice, succeeds on third → proceeds normally, no error
10. Step 4 polling never reaches "complete" → exit 6 after 90s (test with reduced timeout for speed)
11. Step 6 response missing `prompt` field → exit 7
12. Step 7 response missing `metadata.tags` → exit 7
13. `--json` output matches `SunoExtractResult` interface exactly
14. `--check-token` with mocked 200 → exit 0
15. `--check-token` with mocked 401 → exit 2
16. `--setup-help` prints instructions, exits 0, makes zero network calls
17. Token values never appear in stderr/stdout across all tests

### Integration (manual, not automated)

After Ryan ships, Freddie will run the tool once against the real `vault/studio/references/shared/music-cards/audio/1774630111-jacob-collier-improvises-with.mp3` file using the user's real browser-token and device-id. Verify the returned lyrics and style prose match what the user showed in their Suno UI test. This is the end-to-end validation step, manual because it requires live auth.

## Notes for Ryan

1. **The trailing-slash asymmetry is real and non-obvious.** Test both URLs individually. If you template the path as `/api/gen/${clip_id}/${action}/` you will break step 7. Use two distinct URL constants.

2. **Step 5 returns a NEW id.** The clip_id from `initialize-clip` is different from the upload_id. Don't mix them up — steps 6 and 7 need clip_id, not upload_id.

3. **Steps 6 and 7 are "acknowledge and return" pattern.** Suno has already generated the lyrics and style prose during step 4 polling (using Gemini on their backend). Your POST body just acknowledges acceptance; the response is what Suno already computed. This means:
   - Both calls are reasonably fast (~500-800ms observed in HAR)
   - No polling needed after step 6 or 7
   - The response shape is stable and known

4. **S3 upload uses the fields from step 1 EXACTLY.** Do not reorder, do not re-sign, do not modify. Pass them through verbatim, append the `file` field last. Bun's `FormData` handles this correctly if you append fields in the right order.

5. **The HAR showed device-id as a separate header.** The first explorer pass missed this. It's mandatory alongside browser-token on every Suno API call.

6. **Don't over-engineer the URL constants.** Two strings, commented with why the trailing slashes differ. That's it.

7. **The `vault/studio/references/shared/music-cards/audio/1774630111-jacob-collier-improvises-with.mp3` file IS in the repo** (as part of the music cards corpus). You can reference it for manual testing but don't commit any test outputs from it.

8. **If this tool runs under Bun on Windows**, make sure multipart uploads to S3 work correctly. Bun's multipart handling is solid but test it — we've hit weirdness with file path encoding on Windows in the past.

## Task

**Name:** `suno-extract-meta`
**Location:** `src/tools/suno-extract-meta.ts` (single file tool per existing tool runner pattern)

## Background

Session 2026-04-09: user discovered Suno has a new "upload audio" feature that analyzes an uploaded track and auto-populates Lyrics + Style. The extracted values round-trip well — the user tested by regenerating from the extracted values and got a close match to the original audio. This is the first time we've had access to Suno's own semantic interpretation of a track. Previously we only had measured data (BPM, key, spectral features via librosa) plus hand-written Tala craft descriptors.

The flow was reverse-engineered from a real HAR file (`C:/Users/odiem/Downloads/suno.com.har`, not committed to repo, contains real session auth). Two explorer passes mapped the complete 7-step flow with all request/response shapes.

- `content-type: application/json` (on JSON POSTs)

Body: {"extension":"mp3"}

**The clip_id from this step is what steps 6 and 7 use. Do not reuse upload_id.**
